All the obtained 12 + log(O/H) values fall below solar metallicity (consistently with the results by pilyugin07 ), implying an extrapolated SN oxygen abundance of 8.2-8.3 dex (similar to that found in the LMC). The metallicity gradient is found to be unusually flat, likely because this galaxy is interacting with another one (NGC 4485).

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4490 as the 8 Mpc host of SN 2008ax, interacting with NGC 4485 in Arp 269 and having elevated star formation that implies a high supernova rate.

quotes.quote: Supernova SN 2008ax was discovered in galaxy NGC 4490 on 3 March 2008 (Mostardi et al. ) at the position = 12^h 30^m 40.799^s and = 41^ 38' 14.825'' . The host galaxy is 8 Mpc distant (de Vaucouleurs ; we assume an uncertainty of 1 Mpc for the distance), and is dynamically interacting with NGC 4485 (forming the pair Arp 269 ). Likely as a consequence of this interaction, NGC 4490 has a relatively high star formation rate (Viallefond et al. ), which should results in a correspondingly high supernova rate.

sources.summary: NGC 4490 is the primary galaxy in an isolated low-mass pair; the paper models its encounter with NGC 4485, observed morphology and kinematics, and rotation curve.

quotes.quote: In particular, we present a detailed N body simulation that simultaneously reproduces the observed present-day morphology and kinematics of NGC 4490/4485 using ( barnes09 ).

The low mass galaxy pair NGC 4490/4485 (presented in Figure fig:n4490 and with optical centers marked by cyan and magenta ``x"s, respectively) is a slightly more massive analog of the LMC/SMC, with a stellar mass ratio of 8:1 ( clemens98 ) and isolated from any massive galaxy.

Using HI data presented in viallefond80 , elm98 computed a rotation curve for NGC 4490 and found that it peaks at a radius of 6 kpc from the center with v_ rot /sin( i ) 80 , where i is the inclination of the primary in the sky plane, which is 60 .

gmw_00002577.name: host galaxy of GRB 100316D
gmw_00002577.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 100316D is a bright, blue dwarf irregular galaxy [arXiv:1004.2919, arXiv:1704.05509] with a highly disturbed morphology [arXiv:1004.2919]. It features multiple star-forming regions [arXiv:1704.05509] and an extended central region showing signatures of ongoing shock interactions [arXiv:1704.05509].

sources.summary: The paper describes the host galaxy of GRB 100316D as low-redshift, bright blue, highly disturbed, and uses source A spectroscopy to infer subsolar oxygen abundance and a roughly 30 Myr stellar population.

quotes.quote: We report the Swift discovery of the nearby long, soft gamma-ray burst GRB 100316D, and the subsequent unveiling of its low redshift host galaxy and associated supernova.

The host is a bright, blue galaxy with a highly disturbed morphology and we use Gemini South, VLT and HST observations to measure some of the basic host galaxy properties. We compare and contrast the X-ray emission and host galaxy of GRB 100316D to a subsample of GRB-SNe.

Using the measured line fluxes, densities and temperatures we arrive at an electron temperature based oxygen abundance of 12 + log(O/H) = 8.23 0.15 in region A (0.37 times Solar metallicity, assuming a solar oxygen abundance of 12 + log(O/H) = 8.66; Asplund et al. 2004). We note that a similar oxygen abundance has been derived from spectra at the SN site see Chornock . In the spectrum we detect Balmer absorption lines underlying the nebular emission. This is common in GRB host galaxy spectra, and can provide a useful age tracer, providing the spectra have sufficient resolution (e.g. Wiersema et al. in preparation). We fit the H absorption component, and find an approximate age for the dominant stellar population of 30 Myr, assuming continuous star formation Gonzalez .

sources.summary: The paper maps the host of GRB 100316D with IFU data, describing a disturbed dwarf irregular galaxy with multiple H II regions, low-metallicity GRB site, and triggered star formation.

quotes.quote: The low distance, z=0.0591 , of GRB 100316D and its association with SN 2010bh represent two important motivations for studying this host galaxy and the GRB's immediate environment with the Integral-Field Spectrographs like VLT/MUSE. Its large field-of-view allows us to create 2D maps of gas metallicity, ionization level, and the star-formation rate distribution maps, as well as to investigate the presence of possible host companions. The host is a late-type dwarf irregular galaxy with multiple star-forming regions and an extended central region with signatures of on-going shock interactions.

The GRB site is characterized by the lowest metallicity, the highest star-formation rate and the youngest ( 20-30 Myr) stellar population in the galaxy, which suggest a GRB progenitor stellar population with masses up to 20 -- 40 M_ . We note that the GRB site has an offset of 660pc from the most luminous SF region in the host. The observed SF activity in this galaxy may have been triggered by a relatively recent gravitational encounter between the host and a small undetected ( L_ H 10^ 36 erg/s) companion.

sources.summary: The review describes the GRB 100316D host environment as a resolved host complex whose low-metallicity, strongly star-forming GRB site lies within a system showing only a weak metallicity gradient.

quotes.quote: Most recently, Levesque et al. \ (2011) presented a detailed analysis of the GRB 100316D host environment at z = 0.059 . By obtaining longslit spectra of the host complex at two different position angles using LDSS3 on Magellan, we were able to extract spatially-resolved profiles for a number of key diagnostic emission features, thus constructing metallicity and star formation rate profiles across the host that focused on both the specific LGRB explosion site and the diffuse emission of the host complex. Based on this analysis, we determined that GRB 100316D happened near the lowest-metallicity and most strong star-forming region of the host complex. However, this work also revealed only a very weak metallicity gradient within the host complex. Combined with the previous studies of nearby LGRB hosts, we found that, within this small sample, ``host" or ``global" metallicities were comparable to metallicities at the GRB explosion sites (Figure 3), suggesting that global metallicities may indeed be valid proxies for explosion site metallicities in higher-redshift LGRB host galaxy studies.

sources.summary: The paper describes spatially resolved spectroscopy of the GRB 100316D host complex, finding the burst near its lowest-metallicity, strongest star-forming region and only a weak metallicity gradient.

quotes.quote: Most recently, we have presented a detailed analysis of the GRB 100316D host environment at z = 0.059 . [ 40 ] . By obtaining longslit spectra of the host complex at two different position angles using LDSS3 on Magellan, we were able to extract spatially-resolved profiles for a number of key diagnostic emission features, thus constructing metallicity and star formation rate profiles across the host that focused on both the specific LGRB explosion site and the diffuse emission of the host complex. Based on this analysis, we determined that GRB 100316D happened near the lowest-metallicity and most strong star-forming region of the host complex. However, this work also revealed only a very weak metallicity gradient within the host complex.

gmw_00002578.name: NGC 4187
gmw_00002578.overview: NGC 4187 is a galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.031 [arXiv:2105.05396]. It hosts an active galactic nucleus with a compact, flat-spectrum core and a symmetric, two-sided radio jet extending in the west-east direction on parsec scales [arXiv:2105.05396].

sources.summary: The paper associates J1213+5044 with NGC 4187 at z=0.031, finding a symmetric west-east parsec-scale jet, changing spectral index, and compact flat-spectrum core.

quotes.quote: J1213+5044 The source J1213+5044 is associated with the galaxy NGC 4187 at redshift z = 0.031 1999PASP..111..438F . The 150-MHz LOFAR image and 1.4-GHz NVSS image only detected a compact core with a flux density of 178.1 mJy and 96.5 mJy, respectively 1998AJ....115.1693C,2020A&A...642A.107C . J1213+5044 shows a symmetric two-sided structure with jet extending in the west-east direction on parsec scales, in agreement with the previous results 2018ApJ...863..155C . The total flux density spectrum is steep ( ^ 0.15-1.4 = 0.27, ^ 1.4-5 = 1.35) at low frequencies 2020A&A...642A.107C .

The total EVN flux density spectrum is also steep ( ^ 4.99-8.42 _ EVN = 0.61) between 5 and 8 GHz, indicating an emission peak at < 150 MHz. However, the VLBA observation shows a flat spectrum ( ^ 4.99-8.42 _ VLBA = 0.19). The change of spectral index could be related to the change of the emission structure in the core region.
The central component C shows the most compact morphology, highest flux density and flat spectrum ( ^ 4.87-8.37 _ VLBA = 0.18, ^ 4.99-8.42 _ EVN = 0.48) and is identified as the core.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 4187's surface-brightness profile to show that including slightly more inner and outer data in a de Vaucouleurs fit changes effective radius and surface brightness.

quotes.quote: Plotted is the surface brightness profile of NGC 4187 in ^ 1/4 space. A straight line is a good match to the ^ 1/4 law, as is shown by the blue line (fit range indicated by blue arrows). However, a formal fit that includes only a few more interior and exterior points (the red line and red arrows) results in a fit that is 20 larger in effective radius ( r_e ) and an effective surface brightness ( _e ) that is 35 fainter.

gmw_00002579.name: NGC 6702
gmw_00002579.overview: NGC 6702 is an elliptical galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0105435] with a broad surface-brightness profile [arXiv:1303.4710]. While its profile closely resembles a de Vaucouleurs model, a formal Sersic model fit yields an index of 6.2 [arXiv:1303.4710].

sources.summary: The paper finds a possible CO feature in NGC 6702 offset from the optical systemic velocity, but conservatively treats the system as a non-detection pending more sensitive observations.

quotes.quote: For NGC 6702 there appears to be a statistically significant feature at a velocity which is 250 km s^ 1 lower than the optical systemic velocity. Wiklind, Combes Henkel (1995) using data of similar sensitivity do not detect any CO(1--0) emission from this galaxy. If the feature in the NGC 6702 is real it corresponds to an intensity of 0.45 K km s^ 1 and a molecular gas mass of M(H_2)=3.20 10^ 8 M_ . We adopt a conservative approach and assume that no CO(1--0) is detected in this system. More sensitive observations are required to confirm or refute the observed feature.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 6702's broad surface-brightness profile as an example where de Vaucouleurs and Sersic fits differ little but the Sersic fit gives n=6.2.

quotes.quote: Here the surface brightness profile of NGC 6702 is plotted in ^ 1/4 space, and was selected for its large dynamic range in surface brightness that appears ^ 1/4 (i.e., S e rsic n=4 ). The best ^ 1/4 (i.e., linear) fit is shown in blue, with a S e rsic n index of 4 by definition. However, the best S e rsic ^ 1/n model fit (between the two indicated limits) results in a formal fit n index of 6.2. The difference between the fits is negligible with very little curvature at the faint and bright ends, yet a formal fit by a S e rsic ^ 1/n model wildly disagrees with a value of n=4 , and decouples r_e and _e from n .

gmw_00002580.name: SAGE1C J053634.78 722658.5
gmw_00002580.overview: SAGE1C J053634.78 722658.5 is an active galactic nucleus located behind the Large Magellanic Cloud at a redshift of approximately 0.14 [arXiv:1004.1142, arXiv:2004.04531]. It is a disk galaxy that exhibits strong silicate emission but lacks star-formation activity [arXiv:2004.04531].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SAGE 1CJ053634.78 722658.5 as a background quasar with z = 0.14, strong silicate emission, and no detected host-galaxy optical, near-IR, or far-IR component.

quotes.quote: The SAGE-Spec program has also yielded IRS spectra of a number of sources that could not easily be classified based on their broad-band colors. Several of these are background sources. One such background source -- SAGE 1CJ053634.78 722658.5 -- stands out in the IRS spectrum. It has a redshifted spectrum ( z =0.14) that exhibits extremely prominent silicate emission features at 10 and 18  m.

We have analyzed the source and discussed its nature HKW_10_quasar . We argue that the peculiar IRS spectrum and its corresponding broad wavelength energy distribution are indicative of a quasar. We do not detect any emission from the host galaxy; neither the stellar component in the optical or near IR nor the colder ISM in the far-IR see Fig.  fig:quasar ; HKW_10_quasar , and this may thus be another example of a host-less quasar 2005Natur.437..381M .

sources.summary: The paper highlights SAGE1C J053634.78 722658.5 as an abnormal AGN behind the LMC, with redshift 0.1428, strong 10 micron silicate emission, little star formation, and disk-galaxy morphology.

quotes.quote: SAGE1C J053634.78 722658.5 (SAGE0536AGN) is an abnormal AGN discovered behind the LMC in the Spitzer Space Telescope \u201cSurveying the Agents of Galaxy Evolution\u201d Spectroscopy survey ( ) and has since been better characterised with the Southern African Large Telescope (SALT) ( ). Sitting at redshift z=0.1428 0.0001 , the striking feature of this AGN is its uniquely strong silicate emission at 10 m and its lack of star-formation activity. From our use of Galfit ( ) it has been found that this is a disc galaxy instead of an early-type galaxy as previously thought.

gmw_00002581.name: NGC 7603
gmw_00002581.overview: NGC 7603 is a highly variable Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:0709.3076, arXiv:astro-ph/0604468] that exhibits composite optical diagnostics indicating both active galactic nucleus activity and star formation [arXiv:2301.02252]. It is connected by a luminous filament to a higher-redshift companion galaxy, NGC 7603B [arXiv:0902.3151, arXiv:1805.06742], and is surrounded by optical features of possible tidal merger origin [arXiv:1805.06742].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 7603 as a Seyfert galaxy in an anomalous-redshift field, describing its redshift, bridge to NGC 7603B, compact knots, and a radio-galaxy interpretation.

quotes.quote: Here, for the first time, I maintain again the latter, for the ''worst case'' according to Arp, the Seyfert galaxy NGC 7603 and its near celestial neighbours; cf. L o pez-Corredoira and Guti e rrez (2002, 2004). Fig.6A shows the celestial field around NGC 7603, measuring 2' across. The bright Seyfert 1 galaxy NGC 7603, of redshift z = 0.029 (and distance 124 \ Mpc, for H _ 0 = 70 Km/s Mpc), is connected to an object without emission lines, called NGC 7603B, at (larger) redshift z = 0.057, by a curved luminous bridge called ''filament'', of absorption redshift z = 0.030. The filament, in turn, contains two compact emission-line \ `knots' of redshifts z = 0.243 and 0.391, (from OII, H , OIII, OI, NeIII, and H , corresponding to velocity spreads of 10^ 3.2 Km/s), which have been resolved and mapped in (2004), with FWHM 0.3''. Conservatively, the three knotlike objects have been interpreted as HII-galaxies, or NEL galaxies, at much larger distances.

I therefore like to interpret above NGC 7603 as a radio galaxy of which we see a receding ''lobe'' -- \ called ''filament'' above \ - whose red-shifted head is NGC 7603B. The mildly redshifted lobe contains two fast, more strongly redshifted knots: the inner one of slightly higher redshift than the outer one, both of low column density, hence emission-line objects, with a considerable spread of velocities (caused by the jet's impacting at different strengths). Both knots and head are formed from ambient (channel-wall) material swept up by the extremely relativistic pair-plasma jet.

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 7603 as a broad-line Seyfert I galaxy in an anomalous-redshift system, with broad blueshifted Balmer lines, strong Fe II, and a luminous filament connection.

quotes.quote: The main galaxy, NGC 7603, is a broad line Seyfert I galaxy with z=0.0295 and B = 14.04 mag . This galaxy has been studied mainly in relation to its variability . The Balmer lines are unusually broad, show a very complex structure and are blueshifted relative to the local `rest frame' of the AGN by between 1000 and 2000 km/s.

A fact that has attracted attention is the proximity of NGC 7603B, a spiral galaxy with higher redshift ( z=0.0569 ) located 59 arcseconds to the SE of NGC 7603 . The angular proximity of both galaxies and the apparently luminous connection between them makes the system an important example of a possible anomalous redshift association. Arp has claimed that the compact member, NGC 7603B, was somehow ejected from the bigger object. Moreover, there are also two objects overimposed on the filament apparently connecting both galaxies. We identified several emission lines in the spectra of the two knots, and from the emission lines of H , H , OII, doublet OIII, NII-6584 \ we determined their redshifts to be 0.394 0.002 and 0.245 0.002 for the objects closest to and farthest from NGC 7603 respectively .

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 7603 as a Seyfert galaxy with redshift z=0.029, apparently linked by a luminous filament to nearby objects with discordant redshifts.

quotes.quote: Maybe one of the most impressive cases is the Seyfert galaxy NGC 7603 . Seyfert galaxies, less energetic cousins of quasars, are spiral or irregular galaxies containing an extremely bright quasar-like nucleus. The redshift of NGC 7603 is z=0.029 . There is another smaller galaxy, NGC 7603B, with z=0.057 nearby and the two are apparently connected by a narrow luminous filament, NGC 7603B lying at the end of the filament.

The redshift of the filament itself is z=0.030 (the same as for NGC 7603 within the measurement errors which are of the order of 0.001 ). There are two more galaxies lying exactly on the filament: one with the redshift z=0.243 positioned near NGC 7603B, and the another one with z=0.391 positioned near NGC 7603 (see Figure 1a in ).

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Suzaku observations of Markarian 530, a bright Seyfert 1.5 galaxy, finding independent spectral variability modes and noting a higher-redshift companion plus possible tidal merger features.

quotes.quote: A 2012 Suzaku observation of the Seyfert 1.5 galaxy Markarian 530 was analysed and found to exhibit two distinct modes of variability, which were found to be independent from one another. Firstly, the spectrum undergoes a smooth transition from a soft to a hard spectrum. Secondly, the spectrum displays more rapid variability seemingly confined to a very narrow energy band ( 1-3  keV).

Markarian 530 (NGC 7603, z=0.0295 ; hereafter Mrk 530) is a bright (flux in 2-10 keV band of 2 10^ 11 ergs cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 ) Seyfert 1.5 galaxy that has been included in numerous surveys studying various wavelength regimes (e.g. Lal+2004,Singh+2013,Theios+2016,Malkan+2017 ). Mrk 530 has a line-of-sight companion galaxy (NGC 7603B) that is at a higher redshift and connected by filaments observed in optical (e.g. LopezGutierrez2002 ). Features of possible tidal origin surrounding Mrk 530 have been identified in optical images, indicating that the galaxy may have undergone a recent merger event Slavcheva-MihovaMihov2011 .

sources.summary: The paper argues NGC 7603 was effectively a Seyfert 1 during the observations, with variable Seyfert type, a quasar-like mid-infrared spectrum, strong silicate emission, and some PAH starburst contribution.

quotes.quote: Based on the nature of mid-IR spectra of NGC 7603 and NGC 2622 as compared to mid-IR spectra of Seyfert 1s 2006AJ....132..401B , we suggest that these objects are really Seyfert 1s at the time of observations. These sources are known to have variable Seyfert type 1976ApJ...210L.117T,1995ApJ...440..141G and have transitioned back and forth between Seyfert 1 and Seyfert 1.8 states several times in past.

The mid-IR spectrum of NGC 7603 is, however, markedly different than the rest and has characteristics of a type 1 quasar spectrum with strong emission bands at 10 and 18 and very little cool dust emission at longer wavelengths. NGC 7603 does however have some starburst contribution in the form of PAH bands (See Figure 5).

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7603 as an intermediate Seyfert with composite optical diagnostics, starburst-like mid-infrared ratios, and line ratios needing a star-formation component.

quotes.quote: All of the Sy-type sources, with the exception of two targets (NGC 0034 and NGC 7603, flagged with (C) and (D), respectively, in Fig. fig:BPT ) lie in the AGN-dominated area in all three diagrams, according to the criteria from . NGC 7603 is in the composite area between the theoretical demarcation line of maximum starburst from solid curve in Fig. fig:BPT Kewley2006 and the empirical AGN- H ii separation criteria from dotted curve Kauffmann2003 in the / \ versus / \ and / \ versus / \ diagrams.
Interestingly, the intermediate Seyfert NGC 7603, which occupies the mixed-region of the BPT BPT diagram (Fig. fig:BPT ), has / \ <0.1 , close to those of starburst galaxies.
Another is the intermediate Sy NGC 7603 (flagged with (D) in the figure), which lies in the composite area of the BPT, as discussed in Sect. sec:bpt .

sources.summary: The paper finds Mrk 530 compact at 5 GHz with faint northern and southeastern extensions, a steep 5-8.4 GHz spectral index, and nearly all arcsecond-core emission on mas scales.

quotes.quote: Mrk 530 (2316 000) Y : This source was reported to be a compact point source by at 8.4 GHz, whereas our 5 GHz map shows a compact source and a faint (3 ) extension to the north (P.A. 10 ^ ). Comparing our total flux density with the 3.26 mJy found by at 8.4 GHz in June 1991, we derive the spectral index _ 5 GHz ^ 8.4 GHz = 2. Our total flux density is consistent with the measurements of (11.5 mJy), and , a correlated flux density of 11 mJy obtained using a single 275 km baseline at 2.3 GHz. G : Our model-fitting suggests that, in addition to the faint extension to the south-east seen in the map, the compact core also has an extension 1.9 mas to the south-west.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7603 as a highly variable Seyfert galaxy with broad emission lines, large long-term optical and Balmer-line variations, and complex Balmer-decrement behavior.

quotes.quote: However, one of the most variable Seyfert galaxies known so far is NGC 7603. This galaxy with emission-line widths of FWHM 6500 km s ^ 1 varied by a factor of 5 to 10 in the optical continuum and in the Balmer lines over a period of 20 years (Kollatschny et al. ).
A complex behavior of the Balmer decrement as a function of continuum intensity has been seen before, e.g. in the variable Seyfert galaxy NGC 7603 (Kollatschny et al. ).

gmw_00002582.name: NGC 2622
gmw_00002582.overview: NGC 2622 is an active Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:0709.3076] that has transitioned between Seyfert 1 and Seyfert 1.8 states [arXiv:0709.3076]. Its radio emission is dominated by a bright central component with a faint northwest extension [arXiv:astro-ph/0406597], while its X-ray spectrum is characterized by cold absorption [arXiv:2403.13194].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 2622 as a likely Seyfert 1 at observation time, noting past type transitions and a continuum resembling NGC 4151 but with strong PAH bands.

quotes.quote: Based on the nature of mid-IR spectra of NGC 7603 and NGC 2622 as compared to mid-IR spectra of Seyfert 1s 2006AJ....132..401B , we suggest that these objects are really Seyfert 1s at the time of observations. These sources are known to have variable Seyfert type 1976ApJ...210L.117T,1995ApJ...440..141G and have transitioned back and forth between Seyfert 1 and Seyfert 1.8 states several times in past.

1995ApJ...440..141G,1989ApJ...340..190G noted that NGC 7603 and NGC 2622 (also one of our targets), transitioned from a Seyfert 1.8 to a Seyfert 1.5 class possibly as a result of changes in the intrinsic line of sight reddening.
The mid-IR spectrum of NGC 2622 is similar in it's continuum shape to the mid-IR spectrum of NGC 4151 see, and Figure 2 2005ApJ...633..706W , except that NGC 2622 shows strong PAH bands.

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 1218 as dominated by a bright central radio component, with diffuse northwest arcsecond emission and several fainter VLBI blobs aligned with the larger elongation.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1218 (0835+250) Y : found this object to be only slightly resolved at 5 GHz (VLA A array) and 1.4 GHz (VLA B array). Our map shows a diffuse, faint ( 2 ) extension toward the north-west in P.A. 45 ^ . The total flux density of the source is dominated by the bright central component, which is coincident with the optical nucleus Clements83 within the errors. The total flux density of 25.5 mJy measured by us is consistent with the value of 23.0 mJy presented by Ulvestad86 .

sources.summary: The paper finds Mrk 1218 is best described by cold absorption, with a column density near 10^21 cm^-2, consistent with earlier soft-band modeling using a cold absorber.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1218 and Mrk 231 favour the cold absorption scenario, resulting in column densities on the order of 10^ 21 \ cm^ 2 .

Mrk 1218 HG2017 concludes that this source is best fitted by a power law with a cold absorber of N_ H 9 10^ 20 \ cm^ 2 in the soft energy range. This result is consistent with our analysis, (see Table tab:abstest ).

gmw_00002583.name: SDSS J020121.39 + 322829.6
gmw_00002583.overview: SDSS J020121.39+322829.6 is a high-redshift Lyman-alpha emitting starburst galaxy at a systemic redshift of z = 2.8174 [arXiv:1912.04033]. It is strongly gravitationally lensed [arXiv:1811.03628, arXiv:1604.01842], exhibiting a distinctive, elongated blue arc [arXiv:1604.01842] and broad Lyman-alpha absorption [arXiv:1912.04033].

sources.summary: The paper identifies BG0201+3228 as a magnified BELLS GALLERY LAE with cusp-like lensed arcs, z_sys=2.8174, faint stellar absorption features, and broad Ly absorption.

quotes.quote: These are SDSS J020121.39+322829.6, SDSS J074249.68+334148.9, SDSS J075523.52+344539.5, and SDSS J091859.21+510452.5 (hereafter BG0201+3228, BG0742+3341, BG0755+3445, and BG0918+5104, where ``BG'' stands for BELLS GALLERY). The LAEs BG0201+3228, BG0742+3341, and BG0918+5104 are magnified by factors of = 15 3 , 16 3 , and 18 3 , respectively shu2016b , and show similar lensed morphologies, with bright extended arcs and relatively compact counter-images on the other side of the lens in a cusp configuration.

BG0201+ 3228 shows several faint stellar photospheric absorption lines including C iii 1247 , Si iii 1294,1298 , and C iii 1426 . We use the mean of them to derive the rest-velocity of BG0201+3228 as z_ sys = 2.8174 0.0007 . Nebular O iii ] 1666 and C iii ] 1906,1908 emission lines are only barely detected ( 2 ; they fall in regions of relatively strong night sky lines), making their measurements difficult.

On the other hand, in addition to the prominent Ly emission line, BG0201+3228 shows also an underlying broad Ly absorption (see Figure all_laes ). This may indicate that the neutral gas in this LAE is inhomogeneous with both high and low column density H i regions see: mcKinney2019, jaskot2019 , in line with the large covering fraction measured in Section ism ( C_ f (H i ) 0.96 ).

sources.summary: The paper prefers a rounder lens mass profile for SDSS J0201+3228 than S16, while leaving source-property inference for future multi-band data.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0201+3228 Unlike S16, we infer a lens mass profile that is round, q=0.9 , rather than elliptical q=0.7 . For comparison, we have remodelled this dataset with an SIE profile of fixed axis ratio 0.7 and found that the rounder model is preferred by the Bayesian evidence, with a E 900. It should be stressed that both of our independent models based on our two codes disfavour an elliptical mass profile. Moreover, neither the surface brightness profile of the lens nor the lensing configuration from Fig. fig:sample suggests that this is an unusually elliptical lens. It may be that the difference in ellipticity between the S16 model and ours is counteracted by a difference in the external shear strength.
Therefore, any inference of the source properties for this object is left for a future analysis when multi-band data are available.

sources.summary: The paper flags SDSS J020121.39 + 322829.6 as a strong-lens candidate with definitive SDSS imaging evidence, an elongated blue arc southwest of the foreground galaxy, and inclusion in the HST target list.

quotes.quote: SDSS J020121.39 + 322829.6 --One of the two systems that exhibit definitive evidence for strong-lensing features in the corresponding color-composite SDSS images. This particular source shows a distinctive, extremely elongated arc with a relatively bluer color to the southwest of the foreground galaxy (panel (a) in Figure fig:mosaic ). Although the foreground galaxy is a LOWZ instead of CMASS galaxy, the presence of the arc makes it an extremely promising candidate, and hence we include it in the HST target list.

gmw_00002584.name: SDSS J075523.52 + 344539.5
gmw_00002584.overview: SDSS J075523.52+344539.5 is a Lyman-alpha emitting galaxy at a systemic redshift of 2.6342 [arXiv:1912.04033]. It is strongly lensed into a quadruple-image Einstein-cross configuration [arXiv:1811.03628, arXiv:1912.04033] that requires a strong external shear to model [arXiv:1811.03628].

sources.summary: The paper models SDSS J0755+3445 as a quadruple-image lens requiring strong external shear and potential corrections that produce a more compact source.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0755+3445 The modelling of this quadruple image system was possible only through allowing for a strong external shear. In fact, this can already be inferred by noting that the image separation between the central part of the arc and the counter-image is significantly larger than the aperture of the arc, denoting the presence of an external element that perturbs the lensing configuration. Another component of the source is lensed in the two opposite and faint images located along the southwest-northeast diagonal. The galaxy to the southwest direction was found not to have any influence on the lens mass model and was therefore ignored in the modelling procedure.

The LAE source of this system was also reconstructed via the gravitational imaging technique (see VK09). We refer to our follow-up paper for a more detailed description of this procedure. We find that the diffused low-level pixellated convergence corrections which are added to the single power-law model result in a more compact source with a less pronounced tail as shown in Figure fig:contdata .

sources.summary: The paper describes BG0755+3445 as a magnified Einstein-cross LAE with four lensed images, resolved nebular emission, z_sys=2.6342, and a Ly peak near systemic velocity.

quotes.quote: These are SDSS J020121.39+322829.6, SDSS J074249.68+334148.9, SDSS J075523.52+344539.5, and SDSS J091859.21+510452.5 (hereafter BG0201+3228, BG0742+3341, BG0755+3445, and BG0918+5104, where ``BG'' stands for BELLS GALLERY). BG0755+3445 shows an Einstein-cross-like configuration composed of four bright lensed images in the image plane (A to D in Figure fig:2rgb ), all of them observed with the GTC, and is magnified by = 14 2 .

The combined spectrum of BG0755+3445 (covering the four lensed images, A to D in Figure fig:2rgb ) shows a prominent and resolved C iii ] 1906,1908 nebular emission at z_ neb = 2.6345 0.0005 (see Figure fig:fs ), as well as faint stellar photospheric absorption lines, such as C iii 1247 and Si iii 1298 , at z_ stars = 2.6340 0.0007 . We use both nebular emission and stellar absorption lines to measure the systemic redshift z_ sys = 2.6342 0.0008 .

For three LAEs (BG0742+3341, BG0755+3445, and BG1429+1202), however, the Ly line has its maximum intensity very close to, or even consistent with the systemic velocity (see Section sys ). These are the ones that show narrower Ly profiles (230-330 km s ^ 1 FWHM) suggestive of less scatter from low H i column densities and/or that Ly photons freely escape through a clumpy ISM e.g., zheng2014, verhamme2015, verhamme2018, claeyssens2019 .

sources.summary: The paper presents SDSS J0755+3445 as a case where macro-model complexity can mimic substructure, producing diffuse corrections rather than a reliable localized halo detection.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0755+3445 This system is a clear example of how a purely analytical analysis of mass substructure can lead to false detections due to a mis-modelling of the main lens macro model.

The analytical clumpy analysis shows a consistent preference at the 12 level (i.e. E = 72 ) for a model using a NFW halo with a mass of M_ vir ^ NFW = (4.8 0.4) 10^ 10 M _ located at (dx,dy)= (-1.76 0.02, 1.12 0.02) arcsec, relative to the main lens centre. However, no strong positive and localized convergence correction is identified. Low-level diffuse corrections can be seen instead (see Fig.  fig:pot_panel ), which allow for a better focusing of the background source and reduce the positive and negative beating otherwise seen in the low-surface brightness tail of the smooth source. This indicates that the true mass distribution of this system is probably not well described by a single power-law model and that the compactness of the gravitational imaging source is probably due to the extended convergence corrections shown in Fig.  fig:pot_panel .

sources.summary: The paper presents SDSS J075523.52 + 344539.5 as a strong-lens candidate with blue blobs connected by an arc, a fainter counter feature, and likely multiple lensed images.

quotes.quote: SDSS J075523.52 + 344539.5 --The other system that shows strong evidence for strong-lensing features in its color-composite SDSS image. Two distinctive bluish blobs connected by an arc are seen to the northwest and west of the foreground galaxy, with another faint blob to the southeast (panel (b) in Figure fig:mosaic ). Although the detection significance and total flux of its emission line does not meet the selection thresholds, we include this system in the sample because of the suggestion of multiply lensed images.

gmw_00002585.name: SDSS J120159.02 + 474323.2
gmw_00002585.overview: SDSS J120159.02 + 474323.2 is a foreground galaxy identified as a strong gravitational lens candidate [arXiv:1604.01842]. Its color-composite SDSS image displays greenish features surrounding the galaxy, and it is associated with a nearby Lyman-alpha emitting source [arXiv:1604.01842].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J120159.02 + 474323.2 as the sample's highest-flux lens candidate, with a nearby LAE source and greenish SDSS features around the foreground galaxy suggesting strong lensing.

quotes.quote: The observed total flux of the Ly emission is generally between 2 10^ 16 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 and 5 10^ 16 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 except for one (SDSS J1201 + 4743) with an extremely high flux of 1.8 10^ 15 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 . This highest-flux system also happens to have the closest source ( z_S = 2.1258 ). As will be discussed in the following section, this system is one of the two systems with probable strong-lensing features in their SDSS images.

SDSS J120159.02 + 474323.2 --The system with the highest apparent emission-line flux. Its color-composite SDSS image shows some greenish features surrounding the foreground galaxy as a possible indication of strong lensing (panel (d) in Figure fig:mosaic ).

gmw_00002586.name: 2MASX J16110570+0234002
gmw_00002586.overview: 2MASX J16110570+0234002 is an early-type host galaxy that exhibits a bulge-dominated morphology [arXiv:1901.03731]. Archival multi-wavelength data indicate AGN-like emission, and the galaxy experienced a nuclear brightening event in 2017 [arXiv:1901.03731].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2MASX J16110570+0234002 as the early-type host of ASASSN-17cv, reporting dramatic nuclear brightening, AGN-like archival emission, and a bulge-dominated host without an archival central point source.

quotes.quote: This has important implications for the classification of different types of enhanced accretion onto SMBHs. \ was discovered by the All Sky Automated Survey for Supernovae (ASAS-SN ) as ASASSN-17cv on 2017 February 21 in the early-type galaxy 2MASX J16110570+0234002, at \ (ref. Kiyota2017_AT17bgt_Atel ; see Methods SM_sec_obs_img ). The long-term ASAS-SN optical data show that the total emission from the galaxy brightened by a factor of 50 over a period of about two months, with half of the rise occurring within three weeks (see Supplementary Fig. 1). Follow-up Swift observations show that the UV emission increased by a factor of 75 compared to GALEX data from 2004, reaching a luminosity of L_ ( NUV ) 8.9 10^ 44 , and that the X-ray emission increased by a factor of 2 3 compared to ROSAT data from 1990 August (see `Detection and photometric monitoring' and 'Archival multi-wavelength data' in Methods for details on all new and archival data). The archival X-ray luminosity, of L(2 10 ) 7 10^ 42 , and the archival UV to X-ray luminosity ratio are consistent with what is commonly observed in AGN (that is, a UV-to-X-ray spectral slope of _ ox 1.2 ; see Methods SM_sec_mw_data ).

Archival multi-wavelength data The host galaxy of \ is clearly detected in over 9 separate visits of the Panoramic Survey Telescope and Rapid Response System (Pan-STARRS), in all the 5 filters ( grizy ; with 9, 18, 26, 11, and 13 detections in each of these filters). The stacked images, which have ``mean epochs'' ranging 2011 August 17 to 2013 June 8, and are publicly available through the Pan-STARRS database, reveal an early-type host galaxy, with no obvious sign of a central point source. A full GALFIT analysis of the stacked band image, which has a mean epoch of 2011 August 17 (i.e., about 5.5 years before the transient detection), suggests a bulge-dominated morphology. For a single S e rsic fit, we find a S e rsic index of n=5.0^ +0.9 _ 0.6 .

gmw_00002587.name: NGC 7320
gmw_00002587.overview: NGC 7320 is a foreground dwarf spiral galaxy [arXiv:1405.0880, arXiv:2008.01881] that is not a member of the interacting Stephan's Quintet group [arXiv:1206.1234, arXiv:1010.1227]. It exhibits normal disk rotation [arXiv:1105.5840], a well-defined spiral structure [arXiv:2008.01881], and vigorous star formation [arXiv:2008.01881] without signs of tidal distortion [arXiv:1105.5840].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 7320 as a foreground spiral whose clusters minimally contaminate the Old Tail and whose H-alpha data show normal disk rotation without tidal distortion.

quotes.quote: Another spiral galaxy (lower left of the frame), NGC 7320, is known to be a foreground galaxy ( allen80 , moles97 ), with a recessional velocity v_ R = 739 ( UZC ). We do not examine this galaxy in our study.

The Old Tail is mostly obscured by NGC 7320 in our field of view, and as such it is heavily contaminated by the objects of the foreground galaxy NGC 7320. However, a small unobstructed part of that tidal feature, which we are able to observe just southeast of NGC 7320, appears to have minimal contamination from NGC 7320. Indeed, if star clusters of the Old Tail were part of the NGC 7320 then there should be some explanation to such a high, asymmetric concentration of SCCs outside the galaxy (Figure fig:YT_OT ). The most plausible explanation would involve an interaction of NGC 7320 with another galaxy.

However, the H \ study of SQ by Gutierrez2002 did not find any evidence (tidal tails, bridges, etc.) of ongoing or past interactions between NGC 7320 and NGC 7331, the closest galaxy (projected separation is 100 kpc) with similar recession velocity v_ R 800 . Moreover, the observations of H \ in NGC 7320 conducted by williams02 revealed the signature of normal disk rotation, without any signs of tidal distortion.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes foreground dwarf spiral NGC 7320, mapping its star-forming regions, ionization structure, metallicity gradient, and low stellar mass from PINGS integral-field data.

quotes.quote: The foreground galaxy NGC 7320 This galaxy lost its membership to the Stephan's Quintet when it was found to be a foreground object 1961ApJ...134..244B , but its presence in the data frames is unavoidable. Therefore it is necessary to know its characteristics, in order to disentangle its emission from that coming from the real members of the group. The galactic structure is clearly reproduced in the emission-line ratio maps. Fig. 7320figures shows the H emission map, that traces the distribution of the star-forming regions in the galaxy and displays its spiral structure, with multiple regions and complexes of different sizes and morphology. The presence of diffuse emission between the regions is also noticeable.

We plot the metallicity of the regions as a function of their deprojected distances to the nucleus normalized to the optical radius (R _ 25 ; 1991S&T....82Q.621D ) in Fig. 7320metvsdist . A very subtle metallicity gradient is observed between the distances of 0.3 and 0.65 /R _ 25 , while the internal region of the galaxy presents a lower metallicity value, both phenomena described in 2012A&A...538A...8S . Two puzzling regions show high metallicity values although being located at high galactocentric distances, that might suggest an average flat oxygen abundance for the entire galaxy disk.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 7320 as a dwarf interloper, finding southern continuum emission mostly group-borne despite the galaxy's spiral structure, rotation, and star formation.

quotes.quote: NGC 7320 It was previously indicated bnw13b that it is unclear whether the continuum emission in the southern part of the group is bound to the Quintet, or it belongs to the interloper NGC 7320. Detection of a magnetised outflow that follows the neutral hydrogen tail of the Quintet helps to resolve this question at least to the extent that one can be now sure that most of the emission is group-borne. Unfortunately, the lack of detected polarised emission in this area makes it impossible to employ RM Synthesis for further insights.

It is a somewhat surprising result: despite being a dwarf galaxy, the interloper has a well defined spiral structure, and exhibits differential rotation with a sufficiently high maximum rotational velocity williams02 . Vigorous star-formation, a source of turbulence, was also detected xu05 . Therefore, all phenomena that are necessary to support the MHD dynamo mechanism siejkowski11 are present.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7320 as a foreground galaxy in Stephan Quintet, with low recessional velocity, excluded from the interacting group and simulations.

quotes.quote: SQ consists of five galaxies (NGC 7319, NGC 7318a, NGC 7318b, NGC 7317, and NGC 7320, cf. also Fig. morph ) with an estimated distance to earth of 94 Mpc MoMa98,ApXu06 . The fifth galaxy, NGC 7320, is observed to be a much closer foreground galaxy and is not part of the interacting group Sh74,AlSu80,MoSu97 . It has a recessional velocity of 740 km s ^ 1 FaKu99 . The outer tail is extending to the southeast of NGC 7319 and is mostly covered by the foreground galaxy NGC 7320.

sources.summary: The paper discusses NGC 7320 as a likely foreground galaxy with notable H-alpha and radio-contour coincidences visually linking it to the rest of Stephan's Quintet.

quotes.quote: This coincidence reminds us of the case in Stephan's Quintet, and the H _ bridge connecting NGC 7320 to the other galaxies that we have analysed with the same technique: it also happens in this case that the H _ (Fig. 4 of ) with discordant redshift begins exactly where the major component redshift finishes in the bridge connecting NGC 7320 to the rest of the group; in such a case, there was no overlapping of both H _ emissions with different velocities.

There are also other coincidences in Stephan's Quintet, such as radio emission isophotes with 6600--6700 km/s tracing quite exactly the shape of NGC 7320 ( 800 km/s) (Figs 5, 8 of ) and connecting it with the rest of the Quintet, similar to the case of NGC 3067+3C232 . None the less, there are other observations suggesting that there is no connection (see and references therein) and it is quite likely also that NGC 7320 is a foreground galaxy with some coincidences in the system.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 7320 as a foreground dwarf galaxy with symmetric Spitzer-band disk emission and a whole-galaxy SED fit using HII-region plus diffuse dust components.

quotes.quote: The Stephan's Quintet compact group of galaxies (SQ) Among the five galaxies that were observed for the first time by E douard Stephan in 1877, NGC 7320 was later found to be a foreground dwarf galaxy.

The only exception is the emission from the foreground galaxy NGC 7320 which is quite symmetrical in all the Spitzer bands, having a filled disk of emission commonly seen in local universe field galaxies, compatible with the optical/UV appearance not showing any sign of interactions (compatible with its not being a member of SQ).
Foreground galaxy NGC 7320 For the SED fit of the foreground galaxy NGC 7320, we used again the usual combination of HII region plus diffuse photon heated dust emission components.

gmw_00002588.name: CGCG 468-002-NED01
gmw_00002588.overview: CGCG 468-002-NED01 is a galaxy belonging to the interacting pair CGCG 468-002, which is classified as an early-stage luminous infrared galaxy merger [arXiv:1506.09002]. The galaxy hosts an active galactic nucleus, which has been classified as both a Seyfert 2 [arXiv:1301.4015] and a Seyfert 1.9 [arXiv:1506.09002].

sources.summary: The paper newly classifies CGCG 468-002-NED01 as a Seyfert 2, citing optical ratios, mid-infrared [Ne V] emission, and a single-component [O III] fit.

quotes.quote: We first measured the optical line ratios of CGCG 468-002-NED01 since we did not have an optical classification for this galaxy. We classify this galaxy as a Seyfert 2. In and we detected the mid-IR [ Ne v ] lines in this galaxy and measured a high / line ratio. We used both as evidence of the presence of an AGN in this nucleus. The optical classification confirms this.
We found that the fit with one component was only satisfactory for CGCG 468-002-NED01 and NGC 2369.

sources.summary: The paper presents CGCG 468-002 as an early-stage LIRG pair hosting a western Seyfert 1.9 nucleus, with Swift/XRT spectra suggesting absorbed emission and a tentative 0.11c wind.

quotes.quote: The position of the \ source \ evans14 is coincident with the center of CGCG 468-002 NED01 , a local ( z=0.0175 ) galaxy belonging to the interacting pair CGCG 468-002 (projected distance between the center of the galaxies of 29.4 , corresponding to 10 kpc at the source redshift). The high 8-1000 m luminosity of the system, ( L IR / L )=11.2 armus09 , implies a classification as Luminous Infrared Galaxy LIRG, defined as having L IR 10^ 11-12 L ; sanders96 .

The system is an early-stage merger stierwalt13 known to host an AGN optically classified as Seyfert 1.9 see e.g. motch98,vcv01,kollatschny08 . Mainly based on the detection of the high-ionization line \ in the /IRS spectra, the Seyfert 1.9 nucleus has been associated with the western galaxy of the system, coincident with \ see also the optical classification reported by aherrero13 petric11,aherrero12,stierwalt13,stierwalt14 .

The continuum is well described by an intrinsic power law with photon index 1.8 , absorbed by a column of 2.4 10^ 22 . A reflected component is also observed, with a reflection fraction of 1.4 , while a weak soft scattered component ( 2 of scattering fraction) can account for the observed soft emission.

gmw_00002589.name: HS1236+3937
gmw_00002589.overview: HS1236+3937 is an emission-line galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.0184 [arXiv:astro-ph/9909184]. It is a void galaxy with its nearest neighbor at a distance of 8.68 h^-1 Mpc, making it about 12 times more isolated than typical field counterparts [arXiv:astro-ph/9909184].

sources.summary: The paper uses HS1236+3937 as a void-galaxy example, noting z=0.0184, an 8.68 h^-1 Mpc nearest-neighbor distance, and clustering about 12 times more isolated than field ELGs.

quotes.quote: As an example, we illustrate the case of the void galaxy HS1236+3937 (z=0.0184). It has its nearest neighbour ZCAT galaxy at a distance 8.68 h ^ 1 Mpc. At this redshift the average nearest neighbour distances between the field ELGs are 0.75 h ^ 1 Mpc. Thus the galaxy HS1236+3937 differs from its field counterparts by a factor of 12 in its clustering properties.

gmw_00002590.name: NGC 2865
gmw_00002590.overview: NGC 2865 is an E3 elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1406.3574, arXiv:2404.13459] that is widely considered a merger remnant [arXiv:1502.06148, arXiv:1706.06585]. It features a highly disturbed morphology characterized by a kinematically distinct core [arXiv:1502.06148, arXiv:astro-ph/0604452], diffuse dust [arXiv:0706.4467], and a complex system of shells, loops, and tidal tails [arXiv:1406.3574, arXiv:1502.06148].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 2865 as a peculiar E3 shell galaxy with disturbed morphology, tidal features, a past merger, and an unusual H I ring likely connected to that event.

quotes.quote: In this paper we study the surroundings of the elliptical galaxy NGC 2865, which has an unusual quantit of H I gas around it M _ H I 1.2 10 ^9 M _ , schiminovich95 . NGC 2865 is classified as an E3 galaxy in the RC3 catalog Vaucouleurs91 and it satisfies the faber76 relation lake86 . However, deep images of NGC 2865 demonstrate that it is a genuinely peculiar galaxy. The galaxy clearly shows a significantly disturbed morphology. malin83 cataloged NGC 2865 as a shell galaxy: an external shell at about 2 \ east of the nucleus is barely visible both in the NUV and in the HST ACS image.

Also, a faint loop is visible to the northwest of the galaxy while a tail extension can be seen to the southeast Rampazzo07 . These two features are typically associated with galaxy interactions. Stellar spectroscopy and UBV photometry set lower and upper limits to the age of the possible encounter to 1 and 4 Gyr respectively, consistent with major merger models schiminovich95 .

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 2865 as an E3 early-type merger remnant with tidal tails, shells, a young central component, and Ca, Cr, and Mn abundance offsets attributed to merger-driven star formation.

quotes.quote: NGC 2865 is an E3 galaxy originally studied in 1992A&AS...95..489J . Further observations conducted by 2007MNRAS.381..245R , found fine structure that indicated a recent merger history. It has the highest fine structure content ( =6.4 ) among the 3 ETGs, with extended tidal tails and shells surrounding the galaxy. 1986ApJ...306..110F reports that NGC 2865's shells contribute 11-22 of the total luminosity and have colors that resemble disk galaxies. NGC 2865 is thought to have a young, central stellar component formed within the last Gyr and a kinematically distinct core ( 1999astro.ph.10032L , 2006MNRAS.371..633H , 2010MNRAS.401..852R ).

Among the three galaxies we analyzed in this paper, only NGC 2865 shows a chemical difference to literature galaxies without fine structure. We determine that an ETG having fine structure does not necessarily mean its abundance pattern will differ from other ETGs of similar mass. We attribute the abundances differences found in NGC 2865 to the star formation that occurred during its last, major merger.

sources.summary: GALEX imaging shows NGC 2865 has NUV-detected shells and protrusions, a red nearly flat FUV-NUV profile, dust, faint companions, and evidence for recent rejuvenation.

quotes.quote: NGC 2865 . According to Jorgensen92 the galaxy is a bona fide elliptical with a surface brightness profile consistent with a pure ^ 1/4 law. At the same time, NGC 2865 is genuinely peculiar: in deep images the galaxy displays a significantly disturbed morphology. The isophote fit reveals boxy isophotes within 40 and disky isophotes at larger radii Reid94 . The galaxy displays a chaotic system of about 7 shells out to 2.5 \ Fort86 .

While the FUV image is dominated by the presence of the dust visible in the optical image (see the central panel of Figure fig2 ), several of the fine structures detected in this latter are also discernible in the NUV image. Both ``protrusions'' in the West and East sides of the galaxy (evident in the HST-ACS optical image) and some of the shells (labeled 1 and 2 respectively in the top panel) are detected. An external shell at about 2 \ East of the nucleus is barely visible both in the NUV and HST-ACS image.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 2865 as an isolated E3 merger remnant with shells, tails, a kinematically distinct core, steep age and metallicity gradients, and a blue young/intermediate globular-cluster population.

quotes.quote: NGC 2865 is a E3 listed in the reda04 catalogue. It is known for the wealth of substructure (shells, tails, etc.) it possesses michard04,desouza04,tal09,urrutia14 , being most probably a merger remnant hau99 , with a kinematically distinct core hau06 . It presents a remarkably steep age gradient from a central 1.5 Gyr sanchez07,reda07 to 10 Gyr close to 1 R_e ; exhibiting also a steep metallicity gradient reda07 . Its GCS was studied by sikkema06 using HST/ACS, finding a sizeable blue population consistent with a young/intermediate GC population see also trancho14 .

NGC 2865 is widely considered as a merger remnant hau99 . The right panel shows the galaxy surface brightness map with the same scale. The large of number of shells, bluer than the galaxy body, have been discussed elsewhere malin83,fort86 .

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 2865 as a UV-bright merging elliptical with shells and H I tails, finding young metal-rich star-forming regions in its tidal debris likely formed from pre-enriched merger gas.

quotes.quote: NGC 2865 is an UV-bright merging elliptical galaxy with shells and extended H i tails. The regions observed in this work were previously detected using multi-slit imaging spectroscopy. We obtain new multi-slit spectroscopy of six young star-forming regions around NGC 2865, to determine their redshifts and metallicities. The six emission-line regions are located 16-40 kpc from NGC 2865 and they have similar redshifts. They have ages of 10 Myears and an average metallicity of 12+log(O/H) 8.6, suggesting a tidal origin for the regions.

These regions may represent the youngest of the three populations of star clusters already identified in NGC 2865. The high, nearly-solar, oxygen abundances found for the six regions in the vicinity of NGC 2865 suggest that they were formed by pre-enriched material from the parent galaxy, from gas removed during the last major merger.

sources.summary: NGC 2865 is a type II/III-like shell galaxy with blue young central population, incomplete H I disk, and shell/HI structure supporting a gas-rich intruder and spectroscopy target.

quotes.quote: NGC 2865 This galaxy is classified as a type II shell galaxy. However, looking at our residual images this system looks more as if it belongs to the type III class, with lots of irregular features and loops. The core is much bluer (V-I 1.05) than the outer parts (V-I 1.15). This blue colour is related a young stellar population (0.4-1.7 Gyr) which forms a KDC hau . NGC 2865 also contains an incomplete HI disk shimi .

All these simulations use small disk galaxies as the intruders, which is supported by the presence of an HI disk in NGC 2865. The bright shell 2, coinciding with the HI patch, is probably the best candidate for follow-up spectroscopy of our whole sample, because of its high contrast with respect to the galaxy light ( 0.8 mag. higher in V than the galaxy, see Figure 4).

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 2865 as peculiar: its SBF data lie off model grids, indicate intermediate metallicity and old ages, and its V-band image shows diffuse dust.

quotes.quote: NGC 2865 -- It is not unexpected that the only measurement available for this galaxy is significantly out the grid of models, no matter what set of SSP simulations is considered. In fact, as shown in Figure single and discussed in 4.1.2, this galaxy has a peculiar behavior even when other physical properties are taken into account. With these caveats, NGC 2865 data are located between models of Z=0.004 and Z=0.01, on the side of the oldest ages.

Combining optical spectra and spectral synthesis raimann05 have found that the light of this galaxy is mostly dominated ( 70 of the flux) by an t 10 Gyr stellar system (these authors do not differentiate on metallicity). In spite of such age agreement between the results obtained with two different stellar population indicators, we must highlight that the V-band image of NGC 2865 clearly shows the presence of diffuse dust in this galaxy. This finding, together with the peculiar behavior of this galaxy with respect to other data from literature, lead us to reject NGC 2865 in the section dedicated to distance measurements.

sources.summary: The paper presents 2865 as a young elliptical merger remnant with shells, a plume, an outer loop, gas fragments, a young central core, and younger globular clusters.

quotes.quote: 2865 features bright irregular shells, a luminous plume of material, and a faint outer loop, all suggesting that it is the remnant of a relatively recent merger (see Fig. 5 of malin83 and Fig. 1 of hau99 ). This hypothesis is supported by H 1 gas fragments associated with the optical fine structure schiminovich95 , a rapidly rotating small central core with a younger stellar population and gas hau99,serra10 , and a body with unusually blue \ colors. sikkema06 note that in a color histogram the galaxy's GCs seem to contain a subpopulation bluer than normal metal-poor GCs and, hence, likely of much younger age ( 0.5 - 1 Gyr).

The fits, done via Chi-square mapping of the parameter space, yield clear evidence for the presence of intermediate-age clusters in each galaxy. We find that the ages of 1 - 2 Gyr for these globular-cluster subpopulations are consistent with the previously estimated merger ages for the host galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 2865 as a dusty, shelled merger-remnant candidate with measured rotation, a central KDC signature, central dispersion dip, and distorted Hermite profiles.

quotes.quote: NGC 2865 This galaxy contains dust and shells (Reda et al. 2004) and is an excellent candidate for a merger remnant (Hau et al. 1999). Koprolin Zeilinger (2000) measure little or no rotation of 72 15 km/s, _o = 184 5 km/s, and (V/ ) ^ = 0.67 0.14. We measure a maximum velocity of 110 8 km/s and _o = 183 5 km/s. Combined with an assumed ellipticity of 0.2 gives (V/ ) ^ = 1.20 0.12 (see Table 3). The velocity profile shows a clear signature of a KDC in the central 4 arcsec (6.4 kpc) and solid-body rotation in the outer regions.

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 2865 as an isolated recent merger remnant whose unusually blue globular-cluster population may be young and connected to the shell-forming merger.

quotes.quote: NGC 2865 - isolated galaxy ( ), remains of a rotating HI disk ( ). found a KDC and evidence for a young (0.4-1.7 Gyr) stellar population; two possible explanations were given: a starburst or a truncation of the star formation.

In NGC 2865 we find an unusually blue population, which may also be young. In NGC1344 and NGC474 the red cluster population is marginally detected. The radial surface density profiles are more flattened than the galaxy light in the cores. As already known, in NGC3923, which has a high S_N of 5.6, the radial density distribution is more shallower than the diffuse galaxy light. The clusters in NGC 2865 and NGC 7626 provide evidence for formation of a population associated with a recent merger.
NGC 2865 shows all signs of a recent merger event: a very luminous shell and a KDC with evidence of a recent, 0.4-1.7 Gyr, starburst ( ), and an HI disk ( ).

sources.summary: The paper reports that NGC 2865 was observed only in CO(1--0) and yielded the sole confident CO detection in the sample, with an integrated line intensity of 0.55 K km s^-1.

quotes.quote: NGC 2865 was observed in the CO(1--0) emission line only using the SIS 100 GHz receiver because of technical problems with the SIS 115/230 GHz receiver at the time of the observation. The backend was again the low resolution AOS with a bandwidth of 1000 MHz yielding a resolution of about 1.8 km s^ 1 .
Only one confident detection has been measured, that for the CO(1--0) transition in NGC 2865. The integrated line intensity for this transition T_ mb dv =0.55 K km s ^ 1 .

gmw_00002591.name: NGC 5982
gmw_00002591.overview: NGC 5982 is a triaxial elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1309.5908, arXiv:1403.4627] classified as a LINER [arXiv:1309.5908, arXiv:astro-ph/0606385] that exhibits X-ray flux variability [arXiv:1407.0663]. It features a kinematically decoupled central disk [arXiv:astro-ph/0703588], inner shells [arXiv:astro-ph/0703588], dust patches [arXiv:astro-ph/0703588], and UV-emitting blobs that trace young star formation [arXiv:1309.5908].

sources.summary: NGC 5982 is a type I shell galaxy with a kinematically decoupled central disk, inner shells about 1.7 kpc from the center, dust patches, and low dust content consistent with minor merger expectations.

quotes.quote: NGC 5982 NGC 5982 is a Type I shell galaxy (Figure 5). The galaxy is well known for its KDC wagner , recently confirmed using 2D central mapping using OASIS mcdermid and SAURON emsellem . Analogy with the KDCs of Emsellem et al. (2004) indicates that the KDC is probably a rotating central feature, i.e. a disk.

The innermost detectable shell is located about 8'' East. Assuming the galaxy is about two times further away than NGC 3923 (see Table 1), the distance from the centre of this shell is comparable to the inner shell of NGC 3923. Double checking using other methods (unsharp masking and GALFIT peng , which fits symmetric 2D models), show no more inner shells. Unfortunately reliable shell brightness could not be determined, because of their faintness and low contrast.
Although NGC 5982 was listed before as a dust-free galaxy sarzi , we see several patches in the residual frames (two dust patches are visible in Figure E.2 at about 6.5'' E and N.E.).

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5982 as an elliptical with central H-alpha/UV emission, little non-nuclear H-alpha, possible LINER and UV-upturn contributions, and UV blobs tracing young stars.

quotes.quote: NGC 5982 This galaxy has been classified as elliptical. In the R band, the galaxy shows the typical spherical shape of an elliptical. However, in both the H an UV images, the central part shows some emission. In contrast to the other pure elliptical, NGC 5173, NGC 5982 does not seem to have any non-nuclear H emission.

As in the case of NGC 5173, the emission in the central parts may not be due to star formation, especially since NGC 5982 has been classified as a LINER ( Ho1997 ). There may also be a contribution from the UV-upturn phenomenon. In the UV image, there are some blue blobs tracing the formation of young stars.

sources.summary: The paper says NGC 5982 has a normal bright-elliptical-like bimodal globular-cluster color distribution, with radial color gradients driven by changing red and blue cluster fractions.

quotes.quote: We find that the colour distributions for NGC 3923 and NGC 5982 have a bimodal form typical of bright ellipticals, with peaks near V-I=0.92 0.04 and V-I=1.18 0.06 .
NGC 5982 - brightest galaxy of LGG402 (4 members), may be a LINER.

Data on NGC 5982 ( ) also revealed a bimodal distribution; applying our extinction scale to their data gives V-I colours of 0.96 ( 0.03 ) and 1.15 ( 0.03 ). While the blue peak is the same as ours, their red peak is 0.1 magnitude bluer. We attribute this to our much larger GC sample.

sources.summary: The paper notes low central-fiber velocities in NGC 5982, prior evidence for merger-related triaxiality, and a slow-rotator profile with a central low-velocity component.

quotes.quote: In particular, our observations of NGC 474 and NGC 5982 show low velocities in their central fibers whereas higher resolution SAURON kinematics identify KDC's rotating at 50 \ within 1 kpc.

This triaxiality also likes results from merging, as pointed out for NGC 5982 by Oosterloo1994 . In fact, simulations suggest that triaxiality is strongly correlated with the box orbits that result from specifically dry major mergers Jesseit2005, Jesseit2007, Hoffman2009 . In the same way, we have argued that the SRs with rising profiles also show clear signs of triaxiality (as typified by NGC 5982 and NGC 6482). They generally show some evidence of a central LV component that transitions to slow disk-like rotation.

sources.summary: The paper fits NGC 5982 with variable XMM-Newton normalization, deriving soft and hard flux changes over one year, while finding no UV variations.

quotes.quote: NGC 5982 : SMF1 is used to fit XMM -Newton data, with the best representation being when varying Norm_2 (50 ). Flux variations of 11 (49 ) in the soft (hard) band are obtained in a period of one year. UV variations are not found.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 5982 to show that higher-resolution spectroscopy resolves a misaligned, rapidly rotating central component, interpreted as a probable decoupled component or prolate core.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:sau_oas_comp a shows the stellar velocity field of NGC 5982 measured with and . The center appears misaligned in the observations, indicating a probable decoupled component or kinematic twist. This is confirmed by , showing that the misaligned component actually rotates rapidly around the major axis of the galaxy, indicating a prolate core.

gmw_00002592.name: J130612+514407
gmw_00002592.overview: J130612+514407 is a diffuse-looking galaxy at a redshift of 0.2773 [arXiv:1301.4813]. It is classified as the brightest cluster galaxy in its cluster and is suspected to host a quasar core [arXiv:1301.4813].

sources.summary: J130612+514407 is a compact radio source with steep low-frequency spectrum, an SDSS diffuse-galaxy counterpart at z=0.2773, and multiwavelength evidence favoring a quasar-core interpretation.

quotes.quote: J130612+514407 Of the detected sources, J130612+514407 has the steepest spectral index at low frequencies. Without an observation at 6.8 GHz, however, it is impossible to tell if the spectrum remains steep at higher frequencies - the positional offset in the e-EVN observations was only discovered after the e-MERLIN observations had taken place. The e-EVN data show an unresolved point source with an integrated flux density of 7.1 mJy, recovering 30 of the emission detected by the Westerbork array during the same observation, implying structure on intermediate scales at 1.4 GHz. However, the primary beam of the Westerbork tied-array is significantly smaller than the 12.8" offset of the source position from the pointing centre of the e-EVN observation; imaging the source without including the Westerbork array results in a peak flux density of 18.5 mJy and an integrated intensity of 21.6 mJy, recovering almost all of the WSRT-only flux, although it should be noted that these measurements are somewhat uncertain due to the large positional offset. Unlike J072212+291042, J130612+514407 has an optical counterpart in SDSS, J130612.15+514407.0, a diffuse-looking galaxy with an SDSS spectrum giving a redshift of 0.2773.
This object also has counterparts in 2MASS and the ROSAT X-ray catalogue, and is classified as the brightest cluster galaxy in , making the quasar core scenario the most likely.

gmw_00002593.name: J151229+470245
gmw_00002593.overview: J151229+470245 is classified as a peculiar compact steep-spectrum radio source [arXiv:1301.4813]. High-resolution radio observations resolve the galaxy, revealing a core-jet or jet-lobe morphology that may indicate a Type 2 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1301.4813].

sources.summary: J151229+470245 is resolved by e-EVN and e-MERLIN, shows core-jet or jet-lobe structure, and is classified as a peculiar compact steep-spectrum IFRS/radio source.

quotes.quote: The e-EVN observation shows a source elongated in a NE-SW direction, with a peak brightness to total flux density ratio of 1:4. The map obtained with e-MERLIN at 6.9 GHz (Fig. fig1512 ) shows a very different structure. The source is clearly resolved and appears to show a core-jet morphology.

Therefore we may have identified a ``naked jet-lobe'' source without strong AGN core emission. The absence of a compact, self-absorbed core is a sign of a Type 2 AGN, when the jet is viewed at a large angle compared to our line of sight. It is intriguing though, why such an unbeamed object would have a prominent one-sided jet morphology. An alternative interpretation is that we are observing a two-sided jet-lobe with a very weak core within the structure.
Therefore, based on its spectrum and the observed VLBI and e-MERLIN radio structure we may classify J151229+470245 as a peculiar compact steep spectrum radio source (CSS).

gmw_00002594.name: XMMSLJ0619-6553
gmw_00002594.overview: XMMSLJ0619-6553 is a Seyfert I galaxy located at a redshift of 0.0729 [arXiv:1410.1500]. In November 2012, the galaxy exhibited a major X-ray and UV flare, which was likely caused by either a tidal disruption event or an increased active galactic nucleus accretion rate [arXiv:1410.1500].

sources.summary: XMMSLJ0619-6553 is a Seyfert I galaxy whose X-ray/UV flare rose by a factor of 140 and then faded, consistent with either a tidal disruption event or changing AGN accretion.

quotes.quote: In November 2012, X-ray emission was detected from the galaxy (a.k.a. ), a factor 140 times higher than an upper limit from 20 years earlier. Both the X-ray and UV flux subsequently fell, over the following year, by factors of 20 and 4 respectively. Optically, the galaxy appears to be a Seyfert I with broad Balmer lines and weak, narrow, low-ionisation emission lines, at a redshift of 0.0729.

XMMSLJ0619-6553 was not detected in any band above 5 sigma. The 5 sigma upper limits were 2.1 GHz: 984 Jy; 5.5 GHz: 110 Jy; 9.0 GHz: 150 Jy; 17 GHz: 340 Jy; 19 GHz: 486 Jy. In figure fig:lcurve we show the historical X-ray light curve for . Around 2012-11-12 the source flux apparently peaked, reaching a value 140 times higher than an upper limit that can be derived from RASS data taken in 1990.
The flare has either been caused by a tidal disruption event or by an increase in the accretion rate of a persistent AGN.

gmw_00002595.name: 1RXS J013427.2 + + 263846
gmw_00002595.overview: 1RXS J013427.2 + + 263846 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2401.07911], which is a type of active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2401.07911]. High signal-to-noise optical spectroscopy of the source reveals a featureless spectrum [arXiv:2401.07911].

sources.summary: The paper reobserves this BL Lac after a reported emission-line redshift, finds a high-S/N featureless Keck spectrum, and suspects the earlier Mg II line was an artefact.

quotes.quote: 1RXS J013427.2 + + 263846 A low S/N featureless spectrum obtained with the Hobby–Eberly Telescope (HET) has been reported by Shaw13 . A moderate S/N Mayall/KOSMOS spectrum reported by Marchesi18 shows the detection of a bright emission line. If it is interpreted as MgII, this line indicates z = 0.571. We observed the source with Keck/ESI for an exposure time of 6000 sec. The resulting high S/N (135) spectrum (see Fig. fig_spec1 , top panel, on the right) is featureless.

The flux estimated at 5000 in our spectrum is only a factor of 1.5 higher than the flux in the spectrum reported in Marchesi18 , suggesting that the activity state of the AGN at the time of the two observations is not so different. We suspect that the MgII emission line reported in Marchesi18 is an artefact.

gmw_00002596.name: SUMSS J014347 58455
gmw_00002596.overview: SUMSS J014347 58455 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2401.07911], which is a type of active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2401.07911]. Although previous spectra appeared featureless, recent spectroscopy detected calcium absorption lines that establish a redshift of z = 0.3902 [arXiv:2401.07911].

sources.summary: New SALT spectroscopy of this previously featureless BL Lac detects Ca H and Ca I G absorption, yielding a redshift of z=0.3902.

quotes.quote: SUMSS J014347 58455 Previous spectra obtained by SOAR, with a moderate S/N Lan15 , and by EFOSC, with a low S/N Titov17 , are featureless. We performed two separate observations with SALT/RSS at distance of two days with a total exposure time of 4660 sec. The total averaged spectrum with a S/N of 113 is presented in Fig. fig_spec1 (second row panel, on the left). We detected the CaH and CaIG, providing a redshift z = 0.3902 0.0001. With SALT, the CaK line falls into a CCD gap.

gmw_00002597.name: 1RXS J035000.4 + + 064053
gmw_00002597.overview: 1RXS J035000.4 + + 064053 is a BL Lacertae object located at a redshift of z = 0.2730 [arXiv:2401.07911]. It features a luminous host galaxy with an absolute magnitude of M_R = -23.1 and a low jet-to-galaxy flux ratio [arXiv:2401.07911].

sources.summary: Keck spectroscopy detects the host galaxy of this BL Lac at z=0.2730, with low jet-to-galaxy ratio and a luminous host of M_R=23.1.

quotes.quote: 1RXS J035000.4 + + 064053 No previous spectra are available in literature. We observed this source with Keck/ESI for an exposure time of 7000 sec with a S/N of 46. We clearly detect in the spectrum (Fig. fig_spec1 , second row panel, on the right) the host galaxy with several absorption features at z = 0.2730 0.0002. We note that the jet-to-galaxy flux ratio is low at 0.4 0.1, resulting in quite intense absorption lines, allowing for their detection even in a relatively low S/N spectrum. The host galaxy is quite luminous at M_ R = 23.1.

gmw_00002598.name: WISE J053629.06 334302.5
gmw_00002598.overview: WISE J053629.06 334302.5 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1609.08501, arXiv:2401.07911] that exhibits a hard gamma-ray spectral component [arXiv:1609.08501]. High signal-to-noise optical spectroscopy reveals a featureless spectrum, leaving the redshift of the galaxy unknown [arXiv:2401.07911].

sources.summary: The paper resolves a confused gamma-ray field by associating 2WHSP J053628.9-334301 with the hard spectral component, updating its 2WHSP-FGL parameters after likelihood modeling separates it from a nearby FSRQ.

quotes.quote: At this sky position the 2FHL catalog 2FHL reports the source 2FHLJ0536.4-3342, which has been associated with 5BZBJ0536-3343 (=2WHSPJ053628.9-334301) with SED shown in Fig. sed0536m33 . The ray description of this source is very rich, being detected in the 1FGL, 2FGL and 3FGL catalogs (blue/red/green points); pink dots and u.l. correspond to the 2FHL counterpart at E > 50 GeV. For this case, there is a steep+hard component (fig sed0536m33 ) in the ray SED, which may be hard to explain as intrinsic emission from a single source.

Exploring the sky area around 3FGL J0536.4-3347 with the ASDC error circle tool (Fig. 1WHSP0536m33 ) we note that blazar 2WHSP J053628.9-334301 is just outside the ray error ellipse; Also, there is a bright FSRQ (5BZQ J0536-3401) within 15' from the 3FGL source, and it could contribute to the overall ray flux that is observed. A likelihood analysis, assuming two ray sources instead of one (with position corresponding to 5BZQ J0536-3401 and 2WHSP J053628.9-334301), results in a model adjustment with a very large statistical significance for both, as reported in Table solved1 . Each of the resolved sources is associated with a distinct ray spectral component (one steep and one hard) in agreement with expectations: hard for the 2WHSP source and steep for the BZQ object, as shown in Fig. confSED .

sources.summary: VLT/FORS spectroscopy of this BL Lac gives a high-S/N featureless spectrum, so the redshift remains unknown despite earlier NTT/EFOSC observations.

quotes.quote: WISE J053629.06 334302.5 This BL Lac has been observed with NTT/EFOSC by Shaw13 , obtaining a medium S/N ( 40) featureless spectrum. We observed it with VLT/FORS for 1740 s. The resulting spectrum with S/N 170 (Fig. fig_spec1 , third row panel, on the right) has no detectable spectral features. The redshift of WISE J053629.06 334302.5 remains unknown.

gmw_00002599.name: WISE J074627.03 022549.3
gmw_00002599.overview: WISE J074627.03 022549.3 is a blazar [arXiv:1506.07373, arXiv:2401.07911] classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2401.07911]. It exhibits radio emission [arXiv:1506.07373], ultraviolet detection [arXiv:1506.07373], and a featureless optical spectrum that leaves its redshift unknown [arXiv:2401.07911].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NVSS J074627--022549 as the brightest and likely counterpart, with radio emission, gamma-ray-blazar WISE colors, UV detection, and confirmation from the 3FGL ellipse.

quotes.quote: This is a case where there are two X-ray detections inside the Fermi error ellipse (see upper left panel of Figure f2 ), but only one (source 1), which is the brightest, is a likely counterpart. It is in fact the only one with radio emission because it is listed in the NVSS survey as NVSS J074627--022549, with a 20 cm flux density of 12.8 mJy. It has a WISE counterpart with colours W2-W3=2.1 and W1-W2=0.68 , as is typical of gamma-ray blazars. This X-ray source is also UV-detected (GALEXASC J074627.06--022548.9) with far- and near-UV magnitude in the range 20.4--20.8. The other object is dimmer, has no interesting association in other catalogues, and is therefore a less likely association. In the latest Fermi catalogue, 1FHL J0746.3--0225 is associated with 3FGL J0746.4--0225; as is clear in Figure f2 , the 3FGL error ellipse confirms the association of the high-energy detection with source 1 and excludes source 2 as a possible counterpart.

sources.summary: Keck/ESI spectroscopy of this BL Lac produces a high-S/N but featureless spectrum, leaving the redshift of WISE J074627.03 022549.3 unknown.

quotes.quote: WISE J074627.03 022549.3 An intermediate S/N featureless spectrum taken with NOT with an integration time of 1200 s has been reported by Marchesini16 . We performed an observation with Keck/ESI for 6600 s, obtaining a high S/N ( 110) spectrum (Fig. fig_spec1 , bottom panel, on the right), but it is featureless. The redshift of WISE J074627.03 022549.3 remains unknown.

gmw_00002600.name: 1RXS J085802.6 313043
gmw_00002600.overview: 1RXS J085802.6 313043 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2401.07911]. Spectroscopic observations of the active galaxy have detected Ca II H K and Ca I G absorption lines, establishing a redshift of z = 0.7018 [arXiv:2401.07911].

sources.summary: VLT/FORS observations detect Ca II H K and Ca I G in this BL Lac, giving a measured redshift of z=0.7018.

quotes.quote: 1RXS J085802.6 313043 A moderate S/N featureless spectrum obtained at SALT with Goodman High Throughput spectrograph for 1050 s has been presented in Pen17 . We performed three observations with VLT/FORS for a total exposure time of 7830 s, obtaining a S/N = 68 spectrum (Fig. fig_spec2 , top panel, on the right). We are able to detect Ca II HK and Ca I G at a redshift z = 0.7018 0.0002.

gmw_00002601.name: SUMSS J113032 780105
