Furthermore, they both host a spectacular population of luminous globular clusters.

gmw_00002652.name: ID 1
gmw_00002652.overview: ID 1 is a galaxy with a secure 3GHz radio detection [arXiv:2012.02767]. The origin of this radio emission is attributed to AGN radio jets, as its infrared detections are considered unreliable [arXiv:2012.02767].

sources.summary: ID 1 has unreliable infrared detections in COSMOS, no MIR/FIR source in cutouts, and a secure 3GHz radio detection attributed to AGN radio jets.

quotes.quote: ID 1 formally counts multiple detections in Spitzer/MIPS and Herschel /PACS bands but it is flagged as unreliable since it lies in a region of COSMOS affected by incomplete prior coverage and underestimated flux uncertainties. Visual inspection of its MIR and FIR cutouts did not reveal any detection at the source position (see Appendix A). We thus consider the IR detections of ID 1 as unreliable and exclude it from the following test.

Radio As mentioned, one galaxy (ID 1) is securely detected at 3GHz. Given the unphysically high SFR estimated for it ( 10^ 4 M _ yr ^ 1 ), we ascribe the origin of its radio emission to AGN radio jets.

gmw_00002653.name: ID 5
gmw_00002653.overview: ID 5 is a passive galaxy that shows a 24-micrometer detection without a corresponding X-ray detection [arXiv:2012.02767]. This lack of X-rays may be due to strong obscuration hiding the central engine, while its tentative far-infrared signal appears contaminated by nearby projected sources [arXiv:2012.02767].

sources.summary: ID 5 has a significant 24 m detection without X-rays; tentative SPIRE signal is contaminated, while passive spectrum and photometry favor obscuration hiding central-engine photons.

quotes.quote: ID 10, 6 and 5 appear to tentatively show SPIRE/250- and 350 m signal at the source position as revealed by visual inspection. For the latter two, the contamination by nearby projected FIR bright sources due to poor spatial resolution is evident.

Lastly, ID 5 shows a 0.1 mJy 24 m detection at 10 significance which is not matched by an X-ray detection. Given that its spectrum and photometry are both pointing towards a passive nature, we tend to favour the hypothesis for which strong obscuration might be playing a role in hiding X-ray photons from the central engine.

gmw_00002654.name: ID 6
gmw_00002654.overview: ID 6 is a galaxy detected in both X-rays and 24-micrometer emission [arXiv:2012.02767]. Its emission is fully explained by an active galactic nucleus obscured by a dusty absorber, though its best-fitting model also suggests the presence of dust-enshrouded young stars that may be entering a quiescent phase [arXiv:2012.02767].

sources.summary: ID 6 is a hard X-ray counterpart whose 24 m emission matches an obscured-AGN explanation; its best fit also suggests dust-enshrouded young stars near quenching.

quotes.quote: X-rays ID 10, ID 6 and ID 7 have counterparts in the hard X-ray domain with rest-frame luminosities of the order of log(L _ X, 2-10keV [ erg s ^ 1 ] ) 43.7, 44.3 and 44.3 respectively, assuming a photon index =1.4 e.g. Gilli07 .

For these three sources, their expected L(2-10keV) _ 24 m are in agreement with the observed ones within the uncertainties (see Table tab:final ). Therefore, although we cannot reject the scenario in which some level of star formation would contribute to the rest-frame 6 m emission, we conclude that our data are fully explained by an AGN obscured by a dusty absorber.

In some cases, such as for ID 10 and ID 6, the best fitting combination suggests the presence of dust enshrouded young stellar populations. Intriguingly these two galaxies are also detected in X-rays and 24 m, as discussed in sections 6.1 and 6.2, and might be galaxies which just entered into their quiescent phase or with residual SF below the levels probed our FIR data. h Relative strength of spectral breaks The choice of SFH to infer evolutionary parameters intrinsically carries a degeneracy with the functional form adopted.

gmw_00002655.name: ID 7
gmw_00002655.overview: ID 7 is a high-redshift galaxy that lacks strong absorption features in its spectrum [arXiv:2012.02767]. It exhibits hard X-ray emission consistent with an active galactic nucleus obscured by a dusty absorber [arXiv:2012.02767].

sources.summary: ID 7 lacks strong absorption features, requiring combined photometry and grism fitting for redshift confirmation; it also has hard X-ray emission consistent with obscured AGN activity.

quotes.quote: Notably, ID 7 lacks absorption features as strong as for the rest of the sample. This makes the spectrum formally consistent with multiple redshift solutions ( 0.4<z<3.5 ) within 1 confidence. We checked the consistency of the lowest redshift solutions with the available COSMOS2015 photometry placing z>1.5 as a lower-limit, confirmed by its SED which rises in flux from J to H band. The final spectroscopic confirmation of this source was obtained performing the combined fit of its photometry and grism spectrum across the 1<z<3.5 redshift range at high-resolution (dz=0.001). This test confirms the previously derived best fit solution and reduces the error bars on z _ spec at all confidence levels.

X-rays ID 10, ID 6 and ID 7 have counterparts in the hard X-ray domain with rest-frame luminosities of the order of log(L _ X, 2-10keV [ erg s ^ 1 ] ) 43.7, 44.3 and 44.3 respectively, assuming a photon index =1.4 e.g. Gilli07 .

For these three sources, their expected L(2-10keV) _ 24 m are in agreement with the observed ones within the uncertainties (see Table tab:final ). Therefore, although we cannot reject the scenario in which some level of star formation would contribute to the rest-frame 6 m emission, we conclude that our data are fully explained by an AGN obscured by a dusty absorber.

gmw_00002656.name: ID 10
gmw_00002656.overview: ID 10 is a quenched galaxy that is classified as passive, yet it contains a very young and dust-reddened stellar population [arXiv:2012.02767]. The galaxy is considered an active galactic nucleus host near its quenching phase, with hard X-ray emission that is fully explained by an obscured active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2012.02767].

sources.summary: ID 10 is the sample object most consistent with dusty star formation yet classified passive, considered an AGN host near quenching and matched to hard X-ray emission explainable by an obscured AGN.

quotes.quote: The second object showing the largest consistency with a star forming template is ID 10. Despite the addition of photometry rejecting star forming solutions, it should be noted that this object lies in a region of the UVJ diagram where contamination is expected to be more frequent see lustig . Moreover, its best fitting combined mass-weighted age and Av are still pointing towards a very young and dust reddened stellar content ( t_ 50 =0.3^ +0.3 _ 0.1 and Av=1.6^ +0.1 _ 0.3 , respectively). We discuss very young sources with MIPS and Chandra detections in the following sections. We anticipate here that we consider ID 10 in particular an AGN host and likely an object close to its quenching phase.

X-rays ID 10, ID 6 and ID 7 have counterparts in the hard X-ray domain with rest-frame luminosities of the order of log(L _ X, 2-10keV [ erg s ^ 1 ] ) 43.7, 44.3 and 44.3 respectively, assuming a photon index =1.4 e.g. Gilli07 .

For these three sources, their expected L(2-10keV) _ 24 m are in agreement with the observed ones within the uncertainties (see Table tab:final ). Therefore, although we cannot reject the scenario in which some level of star formation would contribute to the rest-frame 6 m emission, we conclude that our data are fully explained by an AGN obscured by a dusty absorber.

gmw_00002657.name: UGC 4805 galaxy
gmw_00002657.overview: UGC 4805 is a spiral galaxy that hosts a classical LINER nucleus [arXiv:2105.02072]. Although the nucleus exhibits LINER-like emission, its primary ionizing source is likely stellar in origin, such as post-AGB stars, rather than an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2105.02072, arXiv:2207.10260].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes UGC 4805's LINER nucleus with MaNGA spectroscopy, deriving nuclear oxygen abundance from gradients, calibrations, and photoionization models and concluding that stellar post-AGB ionization is favored over an AGN.

quotes.quote: In this work, we study the oxygen abundance in relation to the hydrogen abundance (O/H) of the gas phase of the UGC 4805 LINER nucleus. Optical spectroscopic data from the Mapping Nearby Galaxies (MaNGA) survey was employed to derive the O/H abundance of the UGC 4805 nucleus based on the extrapolation of the disk abundance gradient, on calibrations between O/H abundance and strong emission-lines for Active Galactic Nuclei (AGNs) as well as on photoionization models built with the Cloudy code, assuming gas accretion into a black hole (AGN) and post-Asymptotic Giant Branch (p-AGB) stars with different effective temperatures. We found that abundance gradient extrapolations, AGN calibrations, AGN and p-AGB photoionization models produce similar O/H values for the UGC 4805 nucleus and similar ionization parameter values. The study demonstrated that the methods used to estimate the O/H abundance using nuclear emission-line ratios produce reliable results, which are in agreement with the O/H values obtained from the independent method of galactic metallicity gradient extrapolation. Finally, the results from the WHAN diagram combined with the fact that the high excitation level of the gas has to be maintained at kpc scales, we suggest that the main ionizing source of the UGC 4805 nucleus probably has a stellar origin rather than an AGN.

In this work, we present a detailed analysis of the spiral galaxy UGC 4805, an object with a classical LINER nuclear emission and with the largest number of star-forming emission spaxels along the disk.

sources.summary: The paper cites UGC 4805 as a LINER galaxy whose nucleus has consistent oxygen-abundance estimates and whose ionization is favored to be stellar/post-AGB rather than AGN.

quotes.quote: In a recent work, estimated the oxygen abundance of the LINER nucleus of the UGC 4805 galaxy through the extrapolation of the radial abundance gradient, as well as strong emission-line calibrations for AGNs and photoionization models assuming gas accretion into a black hole (representing an AGN) and post-AGB stars with different temperatures. These authors found that all O/H abundance estimations agreed with each other. Although both AGN and post-AGB models were able to reproduce the observational data, the high gas excitation level that must be maintained at kpc scales and the results from the WHAN diagram suggest that the main ionizing source of the UGC 4805 nucleus probably has a stellar origin rather than an AGN.
This result agrees with that found by 2021MNRAS.505.2087K for UGC 4805.

gmw_00002658.name: N271
gmw_00002658.overview: N271 is a low-surface-brightness dwarf galaxy with a central surface brightness of 24.08 mag arcsec^-2 [arXiv:1111.1833]. It is classified as an irregular galaxy with a relatively young stellar population and a high gas mass fraction of 0.87 [arXiv:1111.1833].

sources.summary: The paper identifies N271 as the true low-surface-brightness dwarf host of SN 2009Z and measures its redshift, H I mass, high gas fraction, and young irregular stellar population.

quotes.quote: However, under close inspection of deep archival images, an irregular dwarf galaxy, 2dFGRS N271Z016 hereafter N271, also known as J140153.80-012035.5 in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey; SDSS SDSSDR7 , has been identified as the true host. This galaxy is at the edge of SDSS detection limits -- the SDSS designation was assigned to it prior to the supernova, but the detection is at a very low confidence limit. We classify this galaxy below as a low surface brightness (LSB) galaxy for a concise review of this class of galaxies see for example impeybothun .

We have investigated the dwarf galaxy N271 which is the host of the Type IIb SN 2009Z. It is a low surface brightness (LSB) galaxy with central surface brightness _B = 24.08 0.13 mag arcsec ^ 2 . Using a spectrum obtained with the Effelsberg Radio Telescope we measured a redshift of z = 0.0251 and an H I mass of 2.96 0.12 10^9 M_ . Using SDSS g and band magnitudes to estimate a mass-to-light ratio and therefore a stellar mass, we arrive at the rather high gas mass fraction of f_g = 0.87 0.04 . SED-fitting using UV (GALEX) and optical (SDSS) data points yields a best-fit model of an irregular galaxy with a relatively young stellar population of age 360 Myr.

gmw_00002659.name: A2667 giant arc
aliases_local: brightest central galaxy of the cluster A2667
gmw_00002659.overview: The A2667 giant arc is a star-forming galaxy at redshift z = 1.03 that is gravitationally lensed behind the massive galaxy cluster Abell 2667 [arXiv:1209.3805]. This lensed galaxy exhibits a clumpy morphology in rest-frame ultraviolet and optical wavelengths, with a high velocity dispersion [arXiv:1209.3805].

sources.summary: The paper studies the z=1.03 lensed A2667 giant arc with OSIRIS, characterizing a star-forming, clumpy source with high velocity dispersion and uncertain disk-or-merger morphology.

quotes.quote: We present the spatially resolved emission line ratio properties of a 10 ^ 10 M _ star-forming galaxy at redshift z = 1.03 . This galaxy is gravitationally lensed as a triple-image giant arc behind the massive lensing cluster Abell 2667.

The A2667 giant arc is a three-image lensing system. Covone06 provided the spectroscopic redshift of z = 1.033 for the arc and the lensing models of the Abell 2667 cluster at z = 0.233 . We observed the Abell 2667 giant arc with the OH-Suppressing Infra-Red Imaging Spectrograph (OSIRIS; Larkin06 ) in natural guide star Adaptive Optics (NGSAO) mode on the KECK II telescope at ( _ 2000 , _ 2000 ) = (23:51:40.023, -26:04:51.66).

We find the A2667arc is clumpy in rest-frame UV and optical morphology, and has a high velocity dispersion V _ rot i / 0.73, similar to the turbulent star forming disks at z 2. The current morphology and velocity map is not sufficient to distinguish whether the A2667 arc is a face-on clumpy disk or a merger.

sources.summary: The paper studies the Abell 2667 BCG as a radio-loud massive spheroid with blue substructures, irregular stellar kinematics, composite line emission, star formation, and AGN activity.

quotes.quote: Based on HST and MUSE data, we probe the stellar and gas properties (i.e. kinematics, stellar mass, star formation rate) of the radio-loud brightest cluster galaxy (BCG) located at the centre of the X-ray luminous cool core cluster Abell 2667 ( z = 0.2343 ). The bi-dimensional modelling of the BCG surface brightness profile reveals the presence of a complex system of substructures extending all around the galaxy. Clumps of different size and shape plunged into a more diffuse component constitute these substructures, whose intense `blue' optical colour hints to the presence of a young stellar population. Our results depict the BCG as a massive (M _ 1.38 10^ 11 M _ ) dispersion-supported spheroid ( v _ 150 , _0 216 ) hosting an active supermassive black hole (M _ SMBH 3.8 10^ 9 M _ ) whose optical features are typical of low ionisation nuclear emission line regions.

Although the velocity pattern of the stars in the BCG is irregular, the stellar kinematics in the regions of the clumps show a positive velocity of 100 , similarly to the gas component. An analysis of the mechanism giving rise to the observed lines in the clumps through empirical diagnostic diagrams points out that the emission is composite , suggesting the contribution from both star formation and AGN. We conclude our analysis describing how scenarios of both chaotic cold accretion and merging with a gas-rich disc galaxy can efficaciously explain the phenomena the BCG is undergoing.

sources.original_name: brightest central galaxy of the cluster A2667
sources.summary: The paper models the A2667 brightest central galaxy as a central stellar distribution and uses it to compare SIDM and CDM halo-density predictions against observations.

quotes.quote: We also simulated a galaxy cluster halo with a central stellar distribution similar to the brightest central galaxy of the cluster A2667. A SIDM halo simulated with cross section over mass /m = 0.1 \ provides a good match to the measured dark matter density profile of A2667, while an adiabatically-contracted CDM halo is denser and cuspier. The cored profile of the same halo simulated with /m = 0.5 \ is not dense enough to match A2667.

For the central cluster galaxy we match the results for A2667 as quoted in Newman13a by fitting a Hernquist profile with the same half-light radius to the best-fit dPIE profile, which yields M_ gal = 2.1 10^ 12 and h = 28.5 kpc.

gmw_00002660.name: S7F5 45
gmw_00002660.overview: S7F5 45 is a massive early-type extremely red galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0211394, arXiv:astro-ph/0505467] with a stellar mass exceeding 10^11 solar masses [arXiv:astro-ph/0207352, arXiv:astro-ph/0211394]. Its Sersic index suggests a non-negligible disk component, leading to a probable classification as an S0/Sa galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0610241].

sources.summary: The paper presents S7F5 45 as a massive ERO at redshift about 1.46, younger and dustier than S7F5 254, with a minimum stellar mass of 4e11 solar masses.

quotes.quote: The comparison of the spectra and the photometric data with a grid of synthetic template spectra provides a redshift z 1.22^ 0.07 _ +0.2 for S7F5 254 and z 1.46 0.02 for S7F5 45. The resulting lower limits to their stellar mass are M _ stars ^ min =6 10^ 11 M _ and M _ stars ^ min =4 10^ 11 M _ . The minimum age of the last burst of star formation in S7F5 254 is 3.5 Gyr while it is 0.5 Gyr in S7F5 45 implying a minimum formation redshift z_f 3.5 and z_f 2 for the two EROs respectively.

For this galaxy all the exponentially >0.1 decaying models do not provide acceptable fit to the data (P( ^2 ) <<0.68 ). The single-burst models provide good fit independently on the assumed metallicity. The best-fitting model is the Z=0.4Z _ single-burst, 0.5 Gyr old and with an extinction A _V =1.5 mag (see Table 3). All the other SSP with different metallicities provide good fit as well (0.93 < P( ^2 ) 0.96 ) giving mean ages in the range 0.25 Gyr (Z=2Z _ ) and 0.7 Gyr (Z=0.2Z _ ) and an extinction 1.25 A _V 1.5 . Only the =0.1 (Z=Z _ ) is the exponentially decaying model providing a good fit (P( ^2 ) 0.95 ).

sources.summary: The paper reports that ERO S7F5-45 has a continuum decrease at 0.88-1.0 m, constraining it to 1.3<z<1.35, and estimates its stellar mass above 10^11 solar masses from K-band magnitude.

quotes.quote: We clearly detect in two of them the sharp decrease of the continuum expected at 4000  .

The two analyzed spectra are shown in Figure 1. The decrease happens in the range 0.88-1.0 m in both of them constraining the ERO S7F5-45 at 1.3<z<1.35 and the ERO S7F5-254 at 1.25<z<1.3 . Given the redshift and the absolute K-band magnitudes derived (M _K 27 ) we estimate a lower limit to their stellar mass of M _ star 10^ 11 M _ under the very conservative assumption of M _ star /L _K =0.1 [ M/L ] _ .

sources.summary: The paper treats S7F5 45 as a massive early-type ERO with slight accepted dust extinction and relatively young stellar content, apparently formed when the Universe was about 2.5-3.0 Gyr old.

quotes.quote: In Figure 1 we show the smoothed spectra of the 10 early-type galaxies, two of which (S7F5 45 and S7F5 254) previously analyzed by Saracco et al. (2003), while their broad band photometry is summarized in Table 1.

In most of the cases we could accept A _ V =0 , while only for three galaxies (S2F1 443, S7F5 254, S7F5 45) 0.0 < A_ V < 0.5 . In these latter cases, we verified that the final results do not depend at all from the choice of the extinction law adopted to describe the dust reddening. This was expected since the different extinction curves have about the same shape over the range 0.2 m - 1.0 m corresponding to the rest-frame spectral range used in the present fitting procedure. On the basis of the selected set of acceptable templates, for each galaxy we have defined the range of variability of the parameters which can be derived from the corresponding accepted models: K'-band luminosity, stellar mass content and mean age of their stellar population. The resulting values are summarized in Table 3, where the spanned ranges are reported, together with the spectroscopic measured redshift. The limited range reported for each quantity corresponds to all the models providing acceptable fits to the spectrophotometric properties of the galaxies, being the acceptance criterion based on the accordance within 1 between the observed and the fitted spectral fluxes. In this sense, the wide set of model parameters which have been adopted to perform the fit of the galaxies SED assures that the derived ranges of each measured quantity are robust estimates of their uncertainty. Apart from this uncertainty, each value of M _ K' and M _ star within the acceptable range has its own statistical error, due to the errors in the apparent K'-band magnitude ( 0.1 ), in the redshift measure ( z=0.05 , that can be transformed in 0.1 mag on the absolute magnitude estimate), in the adopted k-correction and dust extinction factor ( 0.1 mag), in the assumed M /L _K' value (15 within a single IMF, 40 if all the IMFs are considered). The corresponding average statistical error on the absolute magnitude M _ K' is 0.2 mag, while the uncertainty on the M _ star estimate is 20 for fixed single IMF and 45 when all the IMFs are considered. No statistical error is affecting the age parameter since it has been estimated only by means of models comparison without involving any measured quantity. The absolute K'-band magnitudes have been calculated assuming the k-corrections derived by redshifting each of the accepted best-fitting templates. In the case of S2F1 443, S7F5 254, S7F5 45, for which the accepted fits of their SEDs assume A _ V >0 , we have taken into account also the extinction applied to the templates to fit the data.

Indeed, the stellar content of 3 of them (S2F5 109, S2F1 511, S7F5 45) appears to be formed when the Universe was about 2.5 - 3.0 Gyr old (i.e., z_f \ 2), 2 galaxies (S2F1 142 and S2F1 389) show the bulk of their stellar content as formed when the Universe was about 2 Gyr old (i.e., z_f \ 3) while the remaining 5 (S7F5 254, S2F1 633, S2F1 357, S2F1 443 and S2F1 527) present a stellar content not younger than 1 Gyr with respect to the Universe (i.e., as formed at z_f > 5).

sources.summary: S7F5 45 is one of three early-type galaxies whose lower Sersic index suggests a non-negligible disk component, leading the authors to classify it probably as S0/Sa.

quotes.quote: For the remaining 3 galaxies, S7F5 254, S7F5 45 and S2F1 443, a Sersic index n<2.5 provides a better fitting to the data suggesting that a non negligible fraction, up to 30 of the light can be distributed on a disk component (see Saglia et al. 1997; van Dokkum et al. 1998). Given the spectrophotometric early-type nature of these galaxies (see Longhetti et al. 2005), we conclude that they probably are S0/Sa galaxies.

The first result of the morphological analysis of our sample of early-type galaxies is that all of them are bulge dominated galaxies. In particular for 6 of them the best fitting S e rsic profile does not improve the fit with respect to the de Vaucouleurs profile providing an index in the range 2.8<n_ fit <4.5 . These galaxies can be considered pure elliptical galaxies. For the remaining 3 galaxies (S7F5 254, S7F5 45 and S2F1 443) the values 1.9<n_ fit <2.3 fit better the data suggesting the presence of a disk component possibly contributing up to 30 to the total light of the galaxy.

gmw_00002661.name: S7F5 254
gmw_00002661.overview: S7F5 254 is an early-type, extremely red galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 1.22 to 1.3 [arXiv:astro-ph/0211394, arXiv:astro-ph/0207352]. It features an old stellar population with a high stellar mass [arXiv:astro-ph/0211394, arXiv:astro-ph/0505467] and is classified as a probable S0/Sa galaxy due to a non-negligible disk component [arXiv:astro-ph/0610241].

sources.summary: The paper identifies S7F5 254 as a massive ERO at redshift about 1.22, with an old stellar population, minimum stellar mass of 6e11 solar masses, and formation redshift at least 3.5.

quotes.quote: The comparison of the spectra and the photometric data with a grid of synthetic template spectra provides a redshift z 1.22^ 0.07 _ +0.2 for S7F5 254 and z 1.46 0.02 for S7F5 45. The resulting lower limits to their stellar mass are M _ stars ^ min =6 10^ 11 M _ and M _ stars ^ min =4 10^ 11 M _ . The minimum age of the last burst of star formation in S7F5 254 is 3.5 Gyr while it is 0.5 Gyr in S7F5 45 implying a minimum formation redshift z_f 3.5 and z_f 2 for the two EROs respectively.

On the contrary S7F5 254 shows a first smooth decrease of the flux at <1.0< <1.3 m followed by the drop which extends at <0.9 m. These features suggest a redshift of S7F5 254 slightly lower than that of S7F5 45 and are typical of old stellar populations.

sources.summary: The paper describes S7F5 254 as an early-type ERO with slight accepted extinction and old stellar content, placing it among objects whose formation redshifts exceed 5.

quotes.quote: In Figure 1 we show the smoothed spectra of the 10 early-type galaxies, two of which (S7F5 45 and S7F5 254) previously analyzed by Saracco et al. (2003), while their broad band photometry is summarized in Table 1.

In most of the cases we could accept A _ V =0 , while only for three galaxies (S2F1 443, S7F5 254, S7F5 45) 0.0 < A_ V < 0.5 . In these latter cases, we verified that the final results do not depend at all from the choice of the extinction law adopted to describe the dust reddening. This was expected since the different extinction curves have about the same shape over the range 0.2 m - 1.0 m corresponding to the rest-frame spectral range used in the present fitting procedure. On the basis of the selected set of acceptable templates, for each galaxy we have defined the range of variability of the parameters which can be derived from the corresponding accepted models: K'-band luminosity, stellar mass content and mean age of their stellar population. The resulting values are summarized in Table 3, where the spanned ranges are reported, together with the spectroscopic measured redshift. The limited range reported for each quantity corresponds to all the models providing acceptable fits to the spectrophotometric properties of the galaxies, being the acceptance criterion based on the accordance within 1 between the observed and the fitted spectral fluxes. In this sense, the wide set of model parameters which have been adopted to perform the fit of the galaxies SED assures that the derived ranges of each measured quantity are robust estimates of their uncertainty. Apart from this uncertainty, each value of M _ K' and M _ star within the acceptable range has its own statistical error, due to the errors in the apparent K'-band magnitude ( 0.1 ), in the redshift measure ( z=0.05 , that can be transformed in 0.1 mag on the absolute magnitude estimate), in the adopted k-correction and dust extinction factor ( 0.1 mag), in the assumed M /L _K' value (15 within a single IMF, 40 if all the IMFs are considered). The corresponding average statistical error on the absolute magnitude M _ K' is 0.2 mag, while the uncertainty on the M _ star estimate is 20 for fixed single IMF and 45 when all the IMFs are considered. No statistical error is affecting the age parameter since it has been estimated only by means of models comparison without involving any measured quantity. The absolute K'-band magnitudes have been calculated assuming the k-corrections derived by redshifting each of the accepted best-fitting templates. In the case of S2F1 443, S7F5 254, S7F5 45, for which the accepted fits of their SEDs assume A _ V >0 , we have taken into account also the extinction applied to the templates to fit the data.

Indeed, the stellar content of 3 of them (S2F5 109, S2F1 511, S7F5 45) appears to be formed when the Universe was about 2.5 - 3.0 Gyr old (i.e., z_f \ 2), 2 galaxies (S2F1 142 and S2F1 389) show the bulk of their stellar content as formed when the Universe was about 2 Gyr old (i.e., z_f \ 3) while the remaining 5 (S7F5 254, S2F1 633, S2F1 357, S2F1 443 and S2F1 527) present a stellar content not younger than 1 Gyr with respect to the Universe (i.e., as formed at z_f > 5). The right panel of the Figure 3 represents these results by showing the age of the Universe at z_ f (squares) and at z (triangles) for each of the galaxies. The length of the line connecting the two points corresponds to the age of their stellar population, while the upper scale helps in relating the age of the Universe at z_ f and z_ f itself. Again it can be noticed that all the galaxies have z_ f >2 , and that for the oldest ones (i.e., those represented by the longest lines, namely S7F5 254, S2F1 633, S2F1 357, S2F1 443 and S2F1 527) the formation redshift is >5 .

sources.summary: The paper reports that ERO S7F5-254 has a continuum decrease at 0.88-1.0 m, constraining it to 1.25<z<1.3, and estimates its stellar mass above 10^11 solar masses from K-band magnitude.

quotes.quote: We clearly detect in two of them the sharp decrease of the continuum expected at 4000  .

The two analyzed spectra are shown in Figure 1. The decrease happens in the range 0.88-1.0 m in both of them constraining the ERO S7F5-45 at 1.3<z<1.35 and the ERO S7F5-254 at 1.25<z<1.3 . Given the redshift and the absolute K-band magnitudes derived (M _K 27 ) we estimate a lower limit to their stellar mass of M _ star 10^ 11 M _ under the very conservative assumption of M _ star /L _K =0.1 [ M/L ] _ .

gmw_00002662.name: S2F1 443

It is an X-ray source that likely hosts a type 2 QSO [arXiv:astro-ph/0610241] and features a structure that suggests a disk component [arXiv:astro-ph/0610241].

sources.summary: The paper describes S2F1 443 as an early-type ERO with borderline color classification, slight accepted extinction, and old stellar content in the group with inferred formation redshift above 5.

Actually, S2F1 527 and S2F1 443 are represented by lower limits in their (R-K') colour and are therefore located in a lower region with respect to their real position in the diagram.

sources.summary: S2F1 443 is the sample's highest-redshift early-type galaxy, has an X-ray source probably hosting a type 2 QSO, and is better fit with a disk-contributing Sersic profile.

quotes.quote: In particular, the n=1 profile provides a fitting significantly worse ( ( ^2_ )>0.3 , d.o.f. 100 ) than the de Vaucouleurs one for 8 early-type galaxies while, for galaxy S2F1 443, the difference is not significant ( ( ^2_ ) 0.01 ). It is interesting to note that S2F1 443 is the highest redshift galaxy of our spectroscopic sample of early-types. Thus, both the surface brightness dimming and the angular scale are larger than for the others. Besides this, this galaxy is ''peculiar'' with respect to the other galaxies since it is X-ray emitting, probably hosting a type 2 QSO (Severgnini et al. 2005).

For the remaining 3 galaxies, S7F5 254, S7F5 45 and S2F1 443, a Sersic index n<2.5 provides a better fitting to the data suggesting that a non negligible fraction, up to 30 of the light can be distributed on a disk component (see Saglia et al. 1997; van Dokkum et al. 1998). Given the spectrophotometric early-type nature of these galaxies (see Longhetti et al. 2005), we conclude that they probably are S0/Sa galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper treats S2F1-443 as an ultramassive dense ETG at z=1.91, noting its X-ray detection, MgII absorption redshift, and velocity-dispersion simulation feasibility.

quotes.quote: We have revised the number density estimated by saracco05 considering only the 6 ETGs at 1.2 < z < 1.6 in the field S2F1 ( 160 arcmin ^ 2 ), thus, excluding the ETG S2F1-443 at z = 1.91 (see Sec.

In facts, the 5 ETGs of our sample were observed with XMM observations and only the source 443 has an X-ray detection severgnini05 . For galaxy 443 we have detected, as expected, the MgII absorption feature. However, we notice that the detection of this absorption line fixes the redshift of this galaxy at z=1.91 slightly higher than the value originally estimated from the AMICI continuum (z =1.70 0.05).

The same set of simulations for the galaxy 443 shows the feasibility of extracting a reliable estimate of velocity dispersion from spectrum covering the blue wavelength range shortward the 4000 in agreement with what found by cappellari09 .

gmw_00002663.name: S2F1 527
gmw_00002663.overview: The galaxy S2F1 527 has color limits that complicate its classification as an early-type galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0505467]. Its stellar content belongs to the oldest group of galaxies, with an inferred formation redshift greater than 5 [arXiv:astro-ph/0505467].

sources.summary: The paper says S2F1 527 has color limits that complicate early-type classification, but its stellar content belongs to the oldest group, with inferred formation redshift above 5.

quotes.quote: At the same time, at least three out of the ten galaxies of our sample (namely, S2F1 527, S2F1 443 and S2F1 389) could not be correctly classified on the basis of the Pozzetti Mannucci criterion, since their colours place them on the border of the classification line. Again it can be noticed that all the galaxies have z_ f >2 , and that for the oldest ones (i.e., those represented by the longest lines, namely S7F5 254, S2F1 633, S2F1 357, S2F1 443 and S2F1 527) the formation redshift is >5 .

Since different stellar ages mean different spectro-photometric properties, we have averaged the spectra of the 10 galaxies after dividing them according to their ages: i) (old) composed by galaxies with age _w 3.0 Gyr (namely S7F5 254, S2F1 527, S2F1 389, S2F1 633, S2F1 443, S2F1 357) and ii) (young) composed by galaxies with age _w < 3.0 Gyr (namely S2F5 109, S2F1 511, S2F1 142, S7F5 45).

gmw_00002664.name: S2F1 389
gmw_00002664.overview: S2F1 389 is an early-type galaxy that lies near the color classification boundary [arXiv:astro-ph/0505467]. The bulk of its stellar content formed when the Universe was approximately 2 Gyr old, placing its formation redshift near 3 [arXiv:astro-ph/0505467].

sources.summary: The paper notes that S2F1 389 lies near the color classification boundary but has demonstrated early-type nature, with stellar content formed around z_f near 3 and grouped among older spectra.

quotes.quote: At the same time, at least three out of the ten galaxies of our sample (namely, S2F1 527, S2F1 443 and S2F1 389) could not be correctly classified on the basis of the Pozzetti Mannucci criterion, since their colours place them on the border of the classification line. Actually, S2F1 527 and S2F1 443 are represented by lower limits in their (R-K') colour and are therefore located in a lower region with respect to their real position in the diagram. This is not the case of S2F1 389 that falls in the SB region of the same diagram, even if quite close to the border line, and whose early-type nature has been demonstrated by Saracco et al. (2005).

Indeed, the stellar content of 3 of them (S2F5 109, S2F1 511, S7F5 45) appears to be formed when the Universe was about 2.5 - 3.0 Gyr old (i.e., z_f \ 2), 2 galaxies (S2F1 142 and S2F1 389) show the bulk of their stellar content as formed when the Universe was about 2 Gyr old (i.e., z_f \ 3) while the remaining 5 (S7F5 254, S2F1 633, S2F1 357, S2F1 443 and S2F1 527) present a stellar content not younger than 1 Gyr with respect to the Universe (i.e., as formed at z_f > 5).

Since different stellar ages mean different spectro-photometric properties, we have averaged the spectra of the 10 galaxies after dividing them according to their ages: i) (old) composed by galaxies with age _w 3.0 Gyr (namely S7F5 254, S2F1 527, S2F1 389, S2F1 633, S2F1 443, S2F1 357) and ii) (young) composed by galaxies with age _w < 3.0 Gyr (namely S2F5 109, S2F1 511, S2F1 142, S7F5 45).

gmw_00002665.name: S2F1 511
gmw_00002665.overview: S2F1 511 is classified as a younger early-type galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0505467]. Its stellar content appears to have formed when the Universe was approximately 2.5 to 3.0 billion years old, and it may have experienced minor subsequent star-forming events [arXiv:astro-ph/0505467].

sources.summary: The paper places S2F1 511 among the younger early-type galaxies, with stellar content apparently formed at z_f around 2 and possibly affected by minor later star-forming events.

quotes.quote: Indeed, the stellar content of 3 of them (S2F5 109, S2F1 511, S7F5 45) appears to be formed when the Universe was about 2.5 - 3.0 Gyr old (i.e., z_f \ 2), 2 galaxies (S2F1 142 and S2F1 389) show the bulk of their stellar content as formed when the Universe was about 2 Gyr old (i.e., z_f \ 3) while the remaining 5 (S7F5 254, S2F1 633, S2F1 357, S2F1 443 and S2F1 527) present a stellar content not younger than 1 Gyr with respect to the Universe (i.e., as formed at z_f > 5).

In particular, S2F5 109, S2F5 45, S2F1 511 for which we derived age_w < 2 Gyr and z_f<3 could just suffered minor star forming events at that redshift while the bulk of their stellar content could be formed at higher redshift.

Since different stellar ages mean different spectro-photometric properties, we have averaged the spectra of the 10 galaxies after dividing them according to their ages: i) (old) composed by galaxies with age _w 3.0 Gyr (namely S7F5 254, S2F1 527, S2F1 389, S2F1 633, S2F1 443, S2F1 357) and ii) (young) composed by galaxies with age _w < 3.0 Gyr (namely S2F5 109, S2F1 511, S2F1 142, S7F5 45).
